questions.question: A retargeting campaign segmented into three ad sets (7-day, 30-day, 90-day window-visitors) shows: 7-day at 12x ROAS / $4 CPA / freq 6.2; 30-day at 6x ROAS / $9 CPA / freq 3.1; 90-day at 2.5x ROAS / $24 CPA / freq 1.4. The team wants to consolidate into one 90-day window. Diagnose the likely outcome.
answer_criteria: The three windows have very different unit economics. Consolidating means Smart Bidding's optimization signal averages across them.

Hypothesis: when consolidated into one ad set with one bidding strategy, Meta will allocate spend toward whichever window's users it predicts will convert. With Cost Cap or tCPA, this typically means the algorithm continues serving the 7-day audience heavily (the highest-converting), but without the structural separation, no longer differentiates creative or messaging.

Lost benefit: with separate ad sets, you can run urgency-led creative for 7-day visitors, education-led for 30-day, brand-rapport for 90-day. Consolidation forces one creative pool across all three windows.

Lost benefit: budget control. Currently each window has dedicated budget. Consolidated, the algorithm would allocate dynamically — likely pushing 80%+ to 7-day (highest predicted ROAS), starving 30 and 90 windows of any meaningful spend.
Verify: pull spend-by-creative-by-window in the existing structure. Confirm that creative differentiation actually drives the per-window performance differences (vs. just the audience).
Action 1 — keep the structure. Multi-window retargeting separation is a feature, not redundancy.
Action 2 — if simplification is needed for management overhead, consolidate 7+30 (similar intent stages) but keep 90-day separate (different stage).
Action 3 — if testing consolidation, do it as a 4-week experiment with clear pre/post metrics, not a one-shot restructure.

Action 4 — set the team's expectation: post-consolidation likely shows: total volume ~same, blended ROAS up (because 7-day dominates), but absolute new-customer/longer-window contribution down (because 30 and 90 don't get budget).
Action 5 — communicate to leadership: 'segment-level architecture preserves long-window contribution; consolidation optimizes for headline ROAS at the cost of mid-funnel coverage'.
Avoid: don't 'simplify' campaign architecture without measuring the strategic value of segmentation.
questions.explanation: Window-segmented retargeting captures different intent stages with different creative. Consolidation collapses the value of stage-aware messaging. Diagnose strategic loss before simplifying.

questions.question: A team's CAPI implementation went live 4 weeks ago. Pixel + CAPI dual-firing. Initial period: dedup rate 99%, EMQ 7.5, all metrics good. Today: dedup rate 91%, EMQ dropped to 6.8, no recent code changes deployed. Diagnose the silent degradation.
answer_criteria: Silent degradation without deploys = something changed in the data flowing through, not the code.
Hypothesis 1: a third-party tracking script (e.g., consent management, A/B test tool, server-side tagging update) is interfering with pixel firing or modifying event_id generation.
Hypothesis 2: a downstream change in Shopify (theme update, app installation) modified the event payload structure, dropping some parameters.

Hypothesis 3: privacy/consent banner was updated; more users now reject cookies, breaking pixel for those users (CAPI still fires server-side, but dedup fails because pixel didn't send the matched event).
Hypothesis 4: ad blocker prevalence in audience increased (e.g., demographic shift), breaking client-side pixel for more users.
Action 1 — diff the deploy log: what was deployed in the last 30 days, even if seemingly unrelated? Theme updates, app installs, JS library updates.

Action 2 — pull pixel-only-fired vs CAPI-only-fired vs both-fired ratios. If pixel-only-fired count dropped, browser-side issue. If CAPI-only-fired count rose, browser-side dropping events server-side picks up.
Action 3 — pull EMQ parameter coverage trends. Which specific parameters degraded? em (email)? fbc (click ID)? client_user_agent? The specific param drop indicates the source.
Action 4 — check consent management tool: did the consent rate drop? Did the consent definition change (e.g., 'ad_storage' now requires explicit opt-in)?
Action 5 — verify pixel firing on a test purchase from a clean browser; verify all expected events fire and parameters are populated.
Action 6 — fix the identified degradation; target dedup ≥99%, EMQ ≥7.5.
Long-term: implement automated daily monitoring of dedup rate + EMQ score with alerts on >5% degradation.
questions.explanation: Silent measurement degradation is one of the most common and under-detected operational issues. The diagnostic systematically rules out code, third-party scripts, consent changes, and parameter coverage.

questions.question: A campaign on Highest Value bidding has these 30-day numbers: $50K spend, $200K reported value, 4x ROAS, 800 purchases, $250 AOV. Last month: $400 AOV. The team is celebrating the 'AOV lift'. Diagnose what's actually happening.
answer_criteria: AOV jump from $250 → $400 is 60% — large for one month with no product/pricing change. Suspicious.
Hypothesis 1: Highest Value's algorithm shifted toward concentrating spend on a high-AOV-skewed sub-audience (e.g., one product line, one geography). The 'lift' is sample-mix change, not actual lift.
Hypothesis 2: a high-value outlier purchase (e.g., one $5,000 wholesale order) is dragging the average up. Pull median AOV — if median is $230 vs mean $400, outliers are driving the metric.
Hypothesis 3: cross-device attribution credited a high-AOV purchase to Meta that came from a different channel.
Verify: pull AOV breakdown by purchase quantile. If top 5% of purchases account for 50% of value, outliers dominate the average.
Verify: pull product-level breakdown. Is one expensive product line over-represented in the new $400-AOV month?
Verify: pull the geographic / device breakdown. Did the audience shift toward higher-AOV geographies?
Action 1 — recompute median AOV (not mean). If median is stable at $230, the 'lift' is outlier-driven, not a real shift.

Action 2 — compare conversion-count vs. value: did total conversions go DOWN while AOV went up? If yes, the algorithm sacrificed volume for high-value buyers — net revenue may not have improved as much as ROAS suggests.
Action 3 — verify with Shopify: is Shopify's AOV (independent of Meta attribution) also showing $400? If Shopify shows $250, the AOV lift is a Meta attribution artifact.
Action 4 — if real: validate that the campaign is acquiring customers profitably at $400 AOV (vs cannibalizing the $250-AOV customer base). Higher AOV is good only if it's incremental, not selection.
Action 5 — adjust expectations: Highest Value tends to skew toward high-AOV outliers; this isn't 'lift', it's selection. The right metric is incremental revenue, not reported AOV.
Action 6 — communicate: 'investigating whether this is a sample-mix effect or genuine value lift; will validate with Shopify-level analysis in 1 week'.
questions.explanation: AOV lifts on Highest Value campaigns are usually selection effects (algorithm cherry-picks high-value buyers from existing demand), not real value creation. The diagnostic separates selection from causation.

questions.question: A team running 4 ASC campaigns reports: ASC1 (Apparel) 5x ROAS, ASC2 (Footwear) 3.5x, ASC3 (Accessories) 4.2x, ASC4 (Home) 2.1x. Total spend $200K/mo split equally. The CFO wants to 'kill ASC4 and reallocate to ASC1'. Walk through your diagnostic before implementing.
answer_criteria: ROAS comparison alone is insufficient for allocation decisions. Walk through what's missing.

Audit margin by product line: ASC4 may have a 50% margin vs ASC1's 25%. ASC4 at 2.1x ROAS = 0.42x contribution; ASC1 at 5x ROAS × 25% = 1.25x. ASC4 may still be unprofitable but the gap to ASC1 is smaller than ROAS suggests.
Audit new-customer rate: ASC4 may be acquiring 80% new customers; ASC1 may be 40%. New-customer acquisition has long-term LTV value not captured in 30-day ROAS.

Audit marginal ROAS: at $50K each, what's the marginal ROAS? ASC4 at 2.1x average might have 1.5x marginal — if ASC1 at $50K has 5x average but 3x marginal, doubling ASC1 to $100K likely yields 3-4x ROAS, not 5x. The ROAS gap shrinks at the margin.

Audit cross-line halo: does Apparel drive Accessory attach rate? If ASC1 (Apparel) feeds Accessory purchases (visible in cross-cart purchase data), its 'true' contribution is higher than its standalone ROAS suggests.

Audit incrementality: ASC4 may serve to a hot retargetable audience whose 2.1x ROAS is largely non-incremental (those users would buy anyway). ASC1's 5x may be more incremental (real demand creation).
Action 1 — pull margin-adjusted contribution per ASC. Recompute: ROAS × margin = contribution multiple. Decide based on contribution, not ROAS.
Action 2 — pull new-customer-rate per ASC. Weight ASC4's contribution by its NCA value if NCA is a strategic priority.
Action 3 — model marginal ROAS at +50% spend on ASC1 (typically 70-85% of average ROAS). The reallocation likely yields less revenue lift than the naive calculation suggests.

Action 4 — counter-recommend to CFO: don't kill ASC4 yet; first improve its ROAS (creative refresh, audience tightening). If after 4 weeks ASC4 doesn't improve, then partial reallocation. Killing entirely loses the optionality.

Action 5 — present the CFO a 3-scenario model: (a) kill ASC4 + 100% to ASC1, (b) cut ASC4 by 50% + 25% to ASC1 / 25% to ASC3, (c) optimize ASC4 in place. Show 90-day projected revenue + new-customer count for each.
Communicate: 'the surface comparison hides 4 important variables; let's decide on contribution + LTV + marginal economics, not ROAS'.
questions.explanation: Allocation decisions across multi-product portfolios require margin, NCA, marginal ROAS, halo, and incrementality analysis — not naked ROAS comparison. The diagnostic forces the right multi-variable framework.

questions.question: A new prospecting campaign was launched 10 days ago. Targeting: broad, 18-65, US, no detailed targeting. Spend $500/day, no conversions yet. The team is panicking. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: 10 days with $5K total spend and 0 conversions is a significant red flag, but the diagnosis depends on conversion event details.
Verify the conversion event: is the pixel/CAPI Purchase event firing correctly elsewhere? Test a manual purchase to confirm.
Verify campaign delivery: how many impressions has the campaign delivered? If <100K impressions in 10 days at $5K, delivery is constrained — diagnose delivery first.
Verify audience: 'broad 18-65 US' is ~250M people. Audience is fine; it's not a targeting issue.
Hypothesis 1: bid cap or CPA target is too aggressive; the algorithm can't find auctions to bid in. Check current bid strategy and target.
Hypothesis 2: creative is hot mess — irrelevant or off-strategy. Check ad-level CTR; if <0.5%, creative is the issue.
Hypothesis 3: tracking is broken on the campaign-specific landing page. Check pixel firing on the landing URL with the Test Events tool.

Hypothesis 4: optimization event mismatch; if optimizing for 'Purchase' but the conversion typically takes 3-5 days post-click, 10 days at low spend means the algorithm has barely seen any conversions to learn from.
Action 1 — verify pixel firing on the campaign's landing page (most common, fastest fix).
Action 2 — verify campaign delivery volume (impressions, reach). If delivery is normal but conversions absent, it's a quality issue, not a delivery issue.
Action 3 — pull ad-level CTR. If <0.7%, swap in stronger creative immediately.
Action 4 — if creative is fine and pixel works: lower the bid strategy aggressiveness (switch to Lowest Cost temporarily) to widen auction eligibility and gather conversion data.

Action 5 — review the conversion event; if Purchase has long lag, optimize for an earlier-funnel event (Initiate Checkout, Add to Cart) initially to give Smart Bidding faster signal, then upgrade after data accumulates.

Action 6 — set the team's expectation: prospecting campaigns can take 7-14 days to show first conversions even when working correctly. 10 days at low spend without a clear failure point means continue diagnosing, not panic-restart.
questions.explanation: New-campaign zero-conversion failures usually trace to one of: pixel break, creative weakness, or over-aggressive bid targets. The diagnostic prioritizes verification over action.

questions.question: A creative test compared two ad concepts (Static vs Video) over 14 days. Static won on ROAS (4.5x vs 3.2x). The team wants to 'shift all budget to static creative'. Diagnose what they should consider before doing so.
answer_criteria: A 1-test, 1-format winner doesn't necessarily justify a category-wide shift. Check several factors.
Sample size: how many conversions per cell? At <500 conversions per cell, the 4.5 vs 3.2 ROAS gap may not be statistically significant.

Placement effects: video and static perform differently across placements (Reels prefers video; Feed equally good for both). Were the cells given equivalent placement options? If video was forced into Reels-only and static into Feed-only, the comparison isn't apples-to-apples.

Audience effects: video may have wider reach but lower CTR due to attention demands; static may convert higher among users already familiar with the brand. This could mean static wins on retargeting but video wins on prospecting.
Creative quality within format: was the static cell using top-tier creatives while video cell had mediocre ones? Format-vs-format comparisons are confounded by within-format quality.
Long-term effects: video typically builds brand recall (delayed value); static delivers immediate response. A 14-day window favors immediate-response formats.
Hypothesis: the right answer might be 'use both' — static for high-intent placements, video for top-funnel awareness — not 'kill video'.
Action 1 — verify statistical significance of the 4.5 vs 3.2 difference. If borderline, extend the test.
Action 2 — break down by placement: was static winning on all placements or just Feed?
Action 3 — break down by audience tier: prospecting vs retargeting performance for each.
Action 4 — pull brand-search-volume data: did video's serving period correspond to higher branded search demand? Video often drives delayed effects not visible in 14-day ROAS.
Action 5 — counter-recommend: don't kill video. Reallocate format mix to ~70% static / 30% video for evergreen, but preserve video for top-funnel and Reels-specific placements.
Action 6 — design follow-up tests with cleaner methodology: equivalent placement allocation, equivalent creative quality, longer measurement window for video (28+ days).
Avoid: sweeping format-level decisions from single-test data.
questions.explanation: Format-vs-format tests are notoriously confounded. The diagnostic forces awareness of placement, audience, quality, and time-window confounds before making category-wide decisions.

questions.question: A brand's last month's MMM (marketing-mix model) attributed 40% incremental contribution to Meta. Concurrent Meta Conversion Lift study showed 22% lift. The CMO is confused — which number is right?
answer_criteria: Both numbers can be 'right' — they measure different things. Diagnose the methodological difference.
MMM measures: total Meta-attributable contribution including base-level effects (brand recall, search-demand creation) — typically 30-60% for established brands at scale.
Conversion Lift measures: incremental conversions in the test cell vs control over the test period. Captures direct lift but misses long-tail brand effects.
The 40% (MMM) is a higher number because it includes downstream demand creation that Lift's short window doesn't capture. The 22% (Lift) is the floor — provably caused conversions in the test window.

Both measurements have biases: MMM relies on time-series modeling and may over-attribute to whatever is correlated with revenue (Meta's spend often correlates with seasonality, sales, etc.). Lift is gold-standard for short-term but expensive and only captures a snapshot.
Triangulate: in healthy measurement programs, MMM > Lift because MMM captures more value. The ratio (40/22 = 1.8x) is reasonable.
Action 1 — explain to CMO: 'these aren't competing — they measure different time horizons and effects. Lift is the conservative floor; MMM is the upper-bound estimate including brand effects'.
Action 2 — for budget decisions: use the average or weighted average. If decision horizon is short (next quarter), weight Lift higher. If long-term (LTV-based planning), weight MMM higher.
Action 3 — for Smart Bidding tCPA target: use Lift-derived iROAS as the conservative target. Setting bidding to MMM's higher number risks over-bidding if MMM over-estimates.

Action 4 — improve both measurements: re-run Lift quarterly with consistent methodology; re-run MMM annually with refreshed inputs. Track the ratio over time — if Lift/MMM ratio shifts dramatically, methodology needs investigation.

Action 5 — communicate decision framework: 'Lift = how many conversions Meta directly caused this quarter; MMM = how many conversions Meta drove this quarter including downstream effects; both are needed'.
questions.explanation: MMM and Lift answer different questions. The CMO's confusion is a measurement-literacy gap. The diagnostic builds the framework for using both.

questions.question: A team's 'always-on' evergreen Meta campaigns have been running for 18 months with stable performance. Last month, they added a 'sale event' campaign for Black Friday. After BF, the always-on campaigns are showing 25% worse ROAS for 3 weeks running. Diagnose the post-event hangover.
answer_criteria: The always-on degradation post-BF is a real pattern. Multiple potential causes need diagnosis.
Hypothesis 1: BF campaigns served to the same audience as always-on, causing post-BF audience exhaustion. Users got 5+ exposures during BF; now they don't engage with always-on creative.
Hypothesis 2: BF creative dominated the algorithm's attention; the algorithm's 'memory' of always-on creative degraded. Algorithm needs to re-warm.
Hypothesis 3: post-BF demand naturally declines; the 25% degradation reflects market reality, not always-on issues.
Hypothesis 4: customers who would have converted in December bought during BF (demand pull-forward). Always-on is hitting a depleted demand pool.
Verify: pull frequency on always-on audiences during and after BF. If BF caused 8x frequency in the audience, post-BF fatigue is real.
Verify: pull demand benchmarks (Google Trends, industry reports) for the post-BF period. Compare current vs. previous-year same-period to separate company effect from market.
Verify: pull retargetable audience size — did the BF spend grow the prospecting pool, leaving more retargeting opportunity? Or did it cannibalize the same audience always-on serves?

Action 1 — measure first: is this pull-forward (demand timing shift) or fatigue (real performance loss)? Pull total customer count YTD vs prior year — if total is up, it's pull-forward; if flat, fatigue.
Action 2 — if pull-forward: accept the 3-4 week reset; performance will normalize as the demand pool rebuilds.
Action 3 — if fatigue: refresh always-on creative to break the BF-association; consider 4-week creative refresh rather than incremental.
Action 4 — long-term: design future event campaigns to use distinct creative + audience layers from always-on, minimizing post-event drag.
Action 5 — set realistic expectations: post-event hangover of 25% for 3-4 weeks is normal; communicate to stakeholders before the event so post-event metrics aren't a surprise.
Document the lesson for next year's BF planning.
questions.explanation: Post-event performance hangovers are common and predictable. The diagnostic separates market-level demand pull-forward from campaign-level fatigue, prescribing different responses.

questions.question: A campaign targets 'US, 25-45, broad' with no other constraints. Spend $2K/day, 30-day data: $32 CPA, 4x ROAS, decent. The team observes that 70% of conversions come from California and proposes 'cut other states to focus on California'. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: 70% from California sounds high but doesn't necessarily mean other states are wasted spend. Need more analysis.

Verify spend distribution: is 70% of spend ALSO going to California, or is California disproportionately efficient? If California has 70% of conversions on 70% of spend, performance is uniform; if 70% conversions on 40% spend, California is unusually efficient.

Hypothesis: California's 70% might reflect (a) population skew (CA = 12% of US population, but high purchase power), (b) brand affinity (early adopters in CA), (c) algorithm-driven concentration (Meta found CA users converting and over-served them).
Hypothesis: cutting other states may shrink the prospecting pool too much. Even if other states have lower per-conversion efficiency, they may have additive volume that California can't replace.
Verify: pull CPA by state. If California is $25 and Texas is $40, the gap matters. If California is $32 and others are $32-$35, similar performance.
Verify: pull marginal returns per state. Has California's CPA risen as spend concentrated there? Marginal CA spend may be more expensive than incremental Texas spend.
Action 1 — segment performance by top-10 states. Identify states clearly underperforming (CPA >50% above target) — those are real candidates for exclusion.
Action 2 — segment by state AND audience signal: maybe California users ARE more efficient in this brand, but the gap isn't 70-30; it's modest.
Action 3 — DON'T concentrate all budget in California immediately. Diminishing returns kick in fast in concentrated geos.
Action 4 — test: run 30-day comparison of (current geographic mix) vs (California-only) at equal budget. Which delivers more total conversions and at what blended CPA?

Action 5 — likely outcome: California-only delivers 50-60% of current conversions at 80-90% CPA, but loses absolute volume. The 'all to California' move sacrifices total customers for headline efficiency.
Action 6 — recommended action: prune the bottom 5-10 states by CPA, not concentrate to California. Keep portfolio diversification.
Communicate: 'concentration risk is real; let's prune underperformers, not concentrate to one geography'.
questions.explanation: Geographic concentration based on observed outcomes is often a selection-effect trap (algorithm found efficient geos and over-served them). The diagnostic separates real geographic performance differences from algorithmic concentration.

questions.question: A campaign on Lowest Cost (no bid constraint) consistently delivers at $35-$40 CPA against an internal target of $30. The team has tried Cost Cap $30 (delivery dropped 70%), Bid Cap $20 (delivery dropped 90%), and Highest Value (CPA went to $50). Diagnose what's actually happening and prescribe.
answer_criteria: The campaign cannot reach $30 CPA with any bidding strategy — that's the diagnostic finding. The $30 target is incompatible with current auction conditions.
Hypothesis 1: the $30 target is unrealistic given the audience, creative, and competitive landscape. The market price for this kind of conversion is $35-$40.
Hypothesis 2: creative isn't strong enough to drive the CVR needed to hit $30 CPA. Improving creative would lower CPA without changing bidding strategy.
Hypothesis 3: landing page CVR is low; the ad-to-landing-page experience leaks conversions. Improving CRO would lower CPA.
Hypothesis 4: optimization event isn't well-tuned. If optimizing for 'Purchase' but the funnel has friction, more conversions could be captured upstream.
Reality check: Cost Cap $30 dropped delivery 70% because the algorithm couldn't find $30-eligible auctions. This is direct evidence the market price is above $30.
Action 1 — accept the data: the target should be raised to $36-$40 (current-market reality) OR the underlying inputs (creative, landing page) must improve to bring CPA down.

Action 2 — invest in the right lever: which has the highest probability of moving CPA from $40 to $30? (a) creative refresh — usually 10-20% CPA improvement possible; (b) landing page optimization — 10-30% CVR lift possible; (c) audience refinement — limited at this stage; (d) bidding tweaks — exhausted.
Action 3 — design a CRO test on the landing page: hypothesis-driven, run for 14-21 days. If CVR improves 20%, CPA drops proportionally to ~$32-$33.
Action 4 — refresh creative with new hooks, formats, and value props. Test 6 new concepts vs current evergreen.
Action 5 — communicate to leadership: 'the $30 CPA target is not achievable with current creative + landing page; either accept $40 CPA or invest in CRO + creative refresh to bring it down'.
Action 6 — set up monthly review of CPA target vs market reality; targets shouldn't be aspirational, they should be achievable with planned interventions.
Avoid: continuing to bounce between bid strategies — the issue isn't bidding.
questions.explanation: When all bidding strategies fail to hit a target, the target is wrong or the underlying inputs (creative, CVR) need to change. The diagnostic forces acceptance of market reality.

questions.question: An ad group has 12 ads. The algorithm has spent 0% on 5 ads (no impressions ever), 5% each on 4 ads, and 80% on the remaining 3 ads. Performance is mediocre. The team wants to know why 5 ads got zero impressions. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: Zero impressions on 5 ads is unusual — it means the algorithm decided very early these ads weren't worth testing. Diagnose why.
Hypothesis 1: the 5 ads were rejected by Meta's policy review. Check Ad Status — 'Disapproved', 'Limited' status would explain zero delivery.
Hypothesis 2: the 5 ads have technical issues — broken landing page link, missing assets, wrong placement compatibility. Check ad preview and link validation.

Hypothesis 3: the 5 ads had very low predicted Quality Score (e.g., due to text-heavy image that historically underperforms, or weak headline). Meta's algorithm filters out predicted-losers before testing.
Hypothesis 4: the 5 ads' format/aspect ratio limits placements. If they're 9:16-only and the ad set is set to all placements, the algorithm may have trouble fitting them.
Verify: open each of the 5 ads → check status, check delivery limitations, check preview on each placement.
Verify: compare ad-quality predictions: was Ad Strength or Quality Ranking flagged 'Below Average'?
Action 1 — check ad status. If rejected, fix the policy issue (often legal/medical/financial claims that Meta auto-flags).
Action 2 — verify the landing page URL is valid for all 5 ads. Broken URLs are a common cause of zero delivery.
Action 3 — verify creative format and placement compatibility. Ensure ad-level creative supports all enabled placements.
Action 4 — if no technical issue: the ads are simply weaker than the others. Decide: kill them and replace with stronger variants, or pause and accept the working 7.
Action 5 — extract learning: review what's different about the zero-impression ads — common features (text-heavy, low contrast, weak headline) tell you what to avoid in future briefs.
Action 6 — long-term: implement an ad-launch checklist that includes status verification, link testing, and placement preview before going live.
Avoid: assuming Meta is 'broken' for not testing all 12 ads — Meta's algorithm intentionally filters weak predictions.
questions.explanation: Zero-impression ads are usually filtered by Meta's algorithm, not platform bugs. The diagnostic walks the systematic checklist before assuming anything else.

questions.question: A team's value-based lookalike (VBL) seeded from top 10% LTV customers historically outperformed standard LAL by 30%. Last 4 weeks, VBL is performing 10% WORSE than standard LAL. Same creative, same budget, no algorithm changes. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: VBL underperforming its baseline + outperformed by standard LAL is a meaningful inversion. Diagnose what's degraded.
Hypothesis 1: the VBL seed audience is stale. If the seed (top 10% LTV) hasn't been refreshed in months, the seed reflects historical buyers, not current high-value patterns.
Hypothesis 2: customer LTV definition has shifted (e.g., subscription product launched, changing what 'high LTV' means). The seed may now optimize for the wrong customer type.

Hypothesis 3: VBL exhausted its high-quality candidate pool. After serving for several months, Meta has saturated the LAL-1% audience and is now serving deeper into LAL-3% / 5% — diluting the value advantage.
Hypothesis 4: standard LAL improved (e.g., better source signal due to recent customer growth) while VBL didn't.
Verify: when was the seed audience last refreshed? Pull the customer file and check upload date.
Verify: check audience size for the VBL — has it grown over time? VBL audiences typically grow as the customer pool grows; if seed is stale, VBL audience size is also stale.
Verify: pull AOV / LTV breakdown of VBL converters vs standard LAL converters in the recent period. If they're now similar, VBL has lost its edge.
Action 1 — refresh the VBL seed. Upload a current top-10% LTV customer file, value-weighted. This is the most likely root cause and easiest fix.
Action 2 — re-evaluate the LTV cutoff: if the business has changed (new product lines, AOV shifts), the 'top 10% LTV' definition may need updating.
Action 3 — give the refreshed VBL 14-21 days to relearn; expect performance to recover toward historical 30% advantage.
Action 4 — set up automated quarterly seed refresh as standard process to prevent this drift.
Action 5 — if refresh doesn't restore performance: VBL may have saturated; consider rotating to a complementary audience signal (custom intent, interest-based prospecting).
Action 6 — document the audience-refresh cadence in operational SOPs.
Communicate: 'audience-signal staleness, fixable via refresh, expect recovery in 2-3 weeks'.
questions.explanation: Audience-signal staleness is a chronic, under-monitored issue. VBL especially decays as the customer base evolves. The diagnostic identifies stale seed as the most likely cause.

questions.question: A new analytics team installed Google Tag Manager last week. Pre-GTM: Meta Pixel + CAPI dual-firing, 99% dedup, EMQ 8.2. Post-GTM: pixel firing dropped 40%, CAPI unchanged, dedup dropped to 60%. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: The 40% pixel-firing drop coinciding with GTM install is almost certainly causal. Diagnose the GTM-to-pixel interaction.

Hypothesis 1: GTM's pixel implementation is firing differently than the previous direct implementation. Common GTM bug: pixel fires only after consent, missing all pre-consent events that the direct implementation captured.
Hypothesis 2: GTM's event triggers are mis-configured. The GTM trigger for Purchase may only fire on certain page views, missing thank-you-page-only conversions.
Hypothesis 3: GTM's consent management integration changed: more events are being suppressed under stricter consent rules.
Hypothesis 4: GTM order of execution: if Meta Pixel loads after the GTM container delay, fast-clicking users may navigate away before pixel fires.
Verify: check GTM container Preview mode for Purchase event. Does the pixel fire on Purchase page? Does it fire reliably?
Verify: compare GTM-fired events (visible in Meta Events Manager Test Events) with prior baseline. Identify which events are dropping.
Verify: check consent management integration — is GTM gating pixel behind consent that previously didn't apply?
Verify: check pixel-load timing — is GTM async-loading the pixel in a way that misses fast-bounce pages?
Action 1 — work with the analytics team to debug the GTM pixel implementation. Goal: restore pixel firing to pre-GTM volumes.
Action 2 — fix consent gating if it's the issue: ensure pixel fires on consent-acceptance OR ensure CAPI captures consent-rejected events server-side.
Action 3 — fix trigger configuration: pixel should fire on Purchase event from any path, not just specific page views.
Action 4 — fix load order: pixel should be high priority in GTM, not async-deferred.
Action 5 — re-test dedup after fix: target ≥95%.
Action 6 — coordinate with analytics team: future infra changes that touch tracking should require Meta Pixel regression testing.
Long-term: institutionalize 'tracking infrastructure changes require analytics + paid-media review' as policy.
Communicate: 'GTM migration broke pixel firing; fixing now; CAPI redundancy partially mitigates but dedup is broken'.
questions.explanation: GTM migrations frequently break pixel implementations through consent gating, trigger misconfiguration, or load timing. The diagnostic systematically rules out each.

questions.question: A team's Meta account has 18 active campaigns. The new agency proposes 'simplifying' to 4 campaigns: Brand-Prospecting, Brand-Retargeting, Performance-Prospecting, Performance-Retargeting. Diagnose what they should consider before consolidating.
answer_criteria: Consolidation can help (signal density, simpler management) or hurt (lost differentiation, lost optimization granularity). Diagnose the 18-campaign structure first.

Audit the current 18: are there sub-segments that would lose distinct optimization in the consolidation? Examples: separate product-line economics, separate customer cohorts (premium vs value), separate geographies with different unit economics.
Audit redundancy: are there 18 campaigns because each is genuinely different OR because the previous team built them ad-hoc without consolidation?
Audit performance: do the 18 campaigns have sufficient conversion volume each? If many are below 30 conv/wk, they're sub-optimal for Smart Bidding — consolidation makes sense.
Audit attribution effects: PMax-style consolidation tends to over-credit certain campaigns. Will the consolidated structure produce reportable per-segment performance?
Hypothesis: the right answer is probably 6-8 campaigns, not 4 or 18. Pure 4-campaign consolidation likely loses important segmentation; pure 18-campaign retention probably has waste.
Action 1 — map the 18 campaigns to economic clusters (product line × funnel stage × customer cohort). Identify clusters with similar economics — those are consolidation candidates.

Action 2 — for each consolidation candidate: pull conversion volume. Below 30 conv/wk per campaign = consolidation likely improves Smart Bidding. Above 100 conv/wk per campaign = each is signal-rich, consolidation provides minimal lift.

Action 3 — design the target structure: typically 6-10 campaigns covering brand + non-brand, prospecting + retargeting, key product/funnel-stage segmentation. Not 4 (over-simplified), not 18 (fragmented).
Action 4 — implement consolidation in stages, not big-bang: combine 2 most-similar campaigns first, measure for 4 weeks, then iterate. Reduces risk of consolidation-shock.
Action 5 — preserve reporting visibility: even if consolidating, use UTM parameters and naming conventions that allow per-segment performance pull.
Action 6 — document why each remaining campaign exists as a distinct entity (the 'reason for separation'). Forcing this discipline prevents future ad-hoc proliferation.
Communicate to agency: 'partial consolidation is better than full; let's do it in stages with clear hypotheses about what each step optimizes'.
questions.explanation: Account-structure simplification is rarely as simple as 'consolidate to 4'. The diagnostic forces multi-dimensional analysis (economics, volume, segmentation needs) before structural changes.

questions.question: A creative-testing program ran 50 ads/month for 6 months. Result: 80% of tested creatives 'failed' (below threshold) and were killed; 20% became evergreen. The team is satisfied with the kill rate. Diagnose what they're potentially missing.
answer_criteria: 80% kill rate sounds aggressive but normal for creative testing. The diagnostic question is whether the testing program is actually generating compounding learning.
Audit 1: Are there documented learnings from the 80% killed? Or are creatives killed and forgotten? If no learning archive, the program isn't compounding.

Audit 2: Are the 'winners' truly diverse hypotheses, or 5 variations on the same theme that all worked? Diversity in winning creative indicates broad learning; homogeneity suggests narrow exploration.

Audit 3: Is the kill threshold appropriate? If killing too fast (e.g., before $1K spend), the algorithm hasn't had enough data to give the creative a fair shot — many killed creatives might have been winners with more runtime.

Audit 4: What's the brief quality? Vague briefs ('try a UGC variation') produce vague learnings. Specific briefs ('test if pricing-led headline outperforms value-prop-led for this product') produce specific learnings.
Hypothesis: the program is producing winners but not necessarily generating compounding insights. Without insight extraction, future tests don't benefit from past results.
Action 1 — institute a creative-test post-mortem template: every test result documents (a) hypothesis tested, (b) result, (c) what we learned for future briefs.
Action 2 — create a searchable archive of past tests + learnings; future briefs reference this archive.

Action 3 — review the kill threshold: is $X spend before kill enough? Pull data on killed creatives — at what spend level are decisions stable? Likely 50-100 link clicks per creative is the floor for confident judgment.
Action 4 — diversify hypothesis space: review the 6-month brief catalog — are tests covering multiple dimensions (hook, format, length, value prop, audience-fit) or repeating themes?
Action 5 — quarterly retrospective: meta-review of which hypotheses validated, which formats compounded, what's the next quarter's bet portfolio?
Action 6 — assign a single creative-strategist owner who maintains the archive and ensures briefs reference past learnings.
Communicate: 'kill rate is healthy; the next maturity step is institutional learning so the program compounds, not just churns'.
questions.explanation: High-volume creative testing is necessary but not sufficient. Without learning archiving and hypothesis discipline, the program produces winners without compounding insight.

questions.question: A team running tCPA $30 has 3 weeks of stable performance. They decide to run a 'bid experiment': split the campaign into two ad sets, one at tCPA $25 (more aggressive) and one at tCPA $35 (more conservative), to learn the elasticity. After 14 days, the $25 ad set has 30% lower volume but 15% better CPA than target. The $35 ad set has 80% higher volume but 20% worse CPA. The team concludes: 'Set tCPA to $30 — current target is right.' Diagnose.
answer_criteria: The conclusion that 'current is right' uses both data points to validate the status quo, but the data actually reveals more.

Insight 1: at $25 target, the algorithm hits the target with a 15% margin (actual ~$28.75) but loses 30% volume. This shows there IS elasticity — tightening the target costs volume but doesn't trade 1-for-1.
Insight 2: at $35 target, volume jumped 80% but CPA missed by 20% (actual $42). The unbounded scaling shows the algorithm is auction-constrained.

Insight 3: the elasticity isn't symmetric. Loosening to $35 produced 80% volume gain; tightening to $25 only lost 30% volume. This asymmetry suggests current $30 target is leaving volume on the table.
Hypothesis: a tCPA of $32-$33 might unlock substantial additional volume (~30-50%) at modestly higher CPA (~$33-$35). The current $30 target is conservative.

Action 1 — recompute the unit economics: at $30 CPA × current volume, total contribution = X. At $33 CPA × +30% volume, total contribution = Y. If Y > X (which it usually is for moderate volume gains), loosening is better.
Action 2 — set up a follow-up test: tCPA $33 for 14 days. Measure volume gain and total contribution.
Action 3 — also test tCPA $28 (between current $30 and tested $25) to map the elasticity curve more granularly.
Action 4 — DON'T conclude 'current is right' from a 2-point comparison. The right tCPA is wherever total contribution is maximized, which requires more granular testing.
Action 5 — long-term: institute quarterly bid-elasticity testing as standard practice. Auction conditions, audience, creative all change — the optimal target shifts.
Action 6 — communicate: 'the experiment showed elasticity; current target may be conservative; next experiment will pinpoint optimal'.
questions.explanation: Bid experiments rarely produce the simple conclusion teams default to. The diagnostic teaches reading the data for elasticity and asymmetry, not validation of the status quo.

questions.question: You manage Meta for a DTC brand: $250K monthly budget, current performance 4.2x ROAS, 60% new-customer rate, 30-day LTV $140 / 90-day LTV $210. AOV $90, COGS 35%. CFO wants you to model the impact of doubling budget to $500K next quarter and present 3 scenarios.
answer_criteria: Frame the model: scaling $250K to $500K rarely produces 2x linear results due to marginal-ROAS decay, audience saturation, creative fatigue acceleration, and CPM inflation.

Scenario A (Aggressive scaling, single step): expect 4.2x → ~3.0x ROAS at +100% spend. Revenue: $500K × 3.0 = $1.5M (vs current $1.05M). Contribution after COGS: $1.5M × 65% - $500K = $475K (vs current $182K). NCA cost rises ~40% as broader audiences enter the funnel.

Scenario B (Staged scaling, +25% per month over 4 months): expect blended ROAS holds ~3.6x by month 4 (less marginal decay due to algorithm relearning at each step). Cumulative revenue advantage: ~10-15% above Scenario A by quarter end.

Scenario C (Scaling + creative + audience expansion): combine 50% budget increase with launching 2 new creative concepts and a value-based-LAL audience refresh. Expected ROAS ~3.8x at 1.5x spend. Best efficiency-volume tradeoff but requires execution capacity.
State assumptions explicitly: marginal ROAS decay function (15-25% per +50% spend); LTV stable; no major macroeconomic changes; creative refresh capacity available.
Quantify NCA: at higher spend, NCA cost rises but absolute new customer count rises too. Model new-customer count: Scenario A ~3,200 (vs current ~1,800); Scenario B ~3,500; Scenario C ~3,400.
LTV-adjusted comparison: at 90-day LTV $210, the right metric is LTV-CAC. Recompute each scenario's LTV-CAC ratio and payback period.
Risk register: each scenario's risks — A: high creative fatigue, ROAS shock; B: slower realization of growth target; C: dependency on new audience signal performing.
Recommendation: Scenario C if creative team has capacity; Scenario B otherwise. Avoid Scenario A unless growth-at-any-cost is the explicit mandate.
Communicate to CFO: present the 3 scenarios with revenue, contribution, NCA count, and risks side by side. Do not recommend a 2x linear projection — that's not a real model.
questions.explanation: Quantitative scaling models require marginal decay assumptions, multi-scenario framing, and stated assumptions. CFO conversations require explicit tradeoffs, not optimistic linear projections.

questions.question: A brand has 4 audience segments running in parallel: VBL-Top10pct ($30K, 5x ROAS, 80% NCA), Standard-LAL ($25K, 4x ROAS, 65% NCA), Broad ($40K, 3.2x ROAS, 75% NCA), Retargeting ($15K, 9x ROAS, 15% NCA). Total $110K, blended 4.3x ROAS. CFO asks: 'Where would you allocate an extra $30K?' Walk through quantitative reasoning.
answer_criteria: Compute cost-per-new-customer (CAC) per segment, since NCA is the underlying value driver: VBL: $30K spend, 5x ROAS × $90 AOV ÷ AOV = ~1,667 conv × 80% NCA = 1,333 new customers; CAC = $30K/1,333 = $22.50. Standard-LAL: similar math = ~$25.50 CAC. Broad: ~$25 CAC. Retargeting: ~$66 CAC.

Model marginal ROAS at +$30K to each segment (typical decay 15-25% per 50%+ spend increase): VBL likely 4.0x at $60K (saturating); Standard-LAL 3.4x at $55K; Broad 3.0x at $70K (capacity for more); Retargeting 6.5x at $45K (audience pool saturation).

Compute marginal cost-per-new-customer (NCA cost on the +$30K): VBL +$30K → ~50 NCAs at marginal $30K spend / NCAs added = ~$300 marginal NCA cost (saturated). Standard-LAL +$30K → similar pattern. Broad +$30K → 600 NCAs at ~$50 marginal NCA cost. Retargeting +$30K → 200 NCAs (mostly returning) at expensive marginal NCA.
Optimal marginal allocation for new-customer growth: $20K to Broad (highest marginal NCA volume), $10K to Standard-LAL (mid risk-reward).
Avoid: VBL is saturating; pumping more there yields diminishing returns. Retargeting is mostly non-incremental at margin.

Stated assumptions: (a) marginal ROAS decay at the cited rates; (b) NCA value is uniform across segments (LTV doesn't differ by acquisition channel); (c) creative inventory exists for the chosen segments.
Validation plan: implement +$20K Broad and +$10K Standard-LAL in 50% steps over 2 weeks. Measure marginal NCA at each step. Adjust if marginal NCA cost exceeds $80 (above LTV-payback threshold).
Risk callout: if Broad's marginal performance is worse than projected, re-allocate within 30 days. Have a fallback plan.
CFO communication: present with NCA-cost framing, not ROAS framing. The scenario where 'highest ROAS gets all the money' would over-allocate to Retargeting and starve growth.
questions.explanation: Multi-segment allocation requires marginal-ROAS modeling per segment + NCA-value framing. Naïve 'highest ROAS wins' allocation almost always over-funds saturated segments and under-funds growth.

questions.question: A brand's MMM attributes 35% incrementality to Meta. Meta's Conversion Lift study (last quarter) showed 25% lift. Internal analyst pitches: 'Average them at 30% and use that for budget decisions.' Walk through why this is wrong and what to do instead.
answer_criteria: The 'average them' approach treats both as point estimates of the same underlying truth — but they measure different things, so averaging doesn't yield a coherent number.
MMM (35%): captures the full Meta-attributable contribution including downstream brand effects (search demand creation, organic lift, brand recall driving direct sessions).
Conversion Lift (25%): captures the directly-measured incremental conversions in test vs control over the test window. Doesn't capture downstream effects.
Both have biases. MMM tends to over-attribute when Meta spend correlates with seasonality. Lift tends to under-attribute by missing long-tail and cross-channel effects.

Right framework: use Lift as the conservative floor for short-term budget decisions (Smart Bidding tCPA target, monthly budget); use MMM for strategic/annual budget allocation (where long-term effects matter).

For Smart Bidding tCPA calibration: target reported CPA = (true incremental CPA target) / Lift incrementality rate. Example: if you want $40 incremental CPA and Lift = 25%, set Meta-reported tCPA at ~$32-$36 (accounting for non-incremental conversions Meta will also report).
For annual planning: use MMM-derived 35% as the planning input; long-term effects matter at this horizon.
For ROI defense to CFO: triangulate. Present both numbers as bounds. 'Meta drives 25-35% of total contribution; we'll plan against the midpoint with sensitivity analysis at both ends.'
Action: don't pick a single number. Build a 2-tier framework: short-term decisions on Lift; long-term on MMM. Document the decision framework so future analysts use both correctly.
Improvement plan: re-run Lift quarterly (more frequent observations); refresh MMM annually. Track the ratio over time — if Lift/MMM ratio shifts dramatically, methodology needs investigation.
questions.explanation: Different measurement methods answer different questions; averaging treats them as the same. The quantitative answer builds a use-case-aware framework rather than a single number.

questions.question: A campaign on Cost Cap $40 has been delivering at $38 CPA, $1,000/day spend, 26 conversions/day. The team wants to test: would Cost Cap $50 generate enough additional volume to be worth the higher CPA? Build the model.
answer_criteria: Model the elasticity: at Cost Cap $40, the algorithm finds 26 conversions/day at avg $38. At Cost Cap $50, the algorithm can pursue auctions where predicted CPA is $40-$50.
Estimate the additional auction inventory: typically a +25% Cost Cap unlocks ~30-50% more auction inventory (depending on auction depth in the audience).
Estimate marginal CPA: the new auctions are by definition more expensive than the original $38. Likely marginal CPA on incremental conversions: $46-$52.

Compute incremental conversions: +30% volume = 8 additional conv/day. Marginal spend = 8 × $48 (mid-marginal) = $384/day additional spend. Total: $1,384/day, 34 conv/day, blended CPA $1,384/34 = $40.7.

Contribution math: at $90 AOV × 35% margin = $31.50 contribution per conversion. Incremental contribution from 8 added conversions = 8 × $31.50 = $252/day. Less the marginal media cost differential (roughly the $48 marginal CPA × 8 = $384 vs avg $40 × 8 = $320 differential = $64). Net incremental contribution = $252 - $64 = $188/day.
Decision: positive contribution makes the test worthwhile. $188/day = ~$5,640/month additional contribution.
Risk callout: if marginal CPA turns out higher than $52, contribution shrinks. If much higher (>$60), it becomes negative.
Test design: implement Cost Cap $50 for 14 days; measure actual marginal CPA on the +volume; decide based on observed economics.
Stated assumptions: (a) marginal auction inventory elasticity at +25% Cost Cap; (b) AOV stable; (c) margin stable; (d) no creative or audience refresh confounds the period.
Recommendation: run the test. Expected positive contribution ~$5,600/month at modest risk; downside is bounded by ability to revert.
questions.explanation: Bid-cap elasticity decisions require modeling marginal auction inventory + marginal CPA + contribution math. Numerical modeling distinguishes 'worth testing' from 'don't bother'.

questions.question: A team's 6 ASCs have these economics: ASC1 $30K spend / 4x ROAS / 70% NCA; ASC2 $25K / 5x / 50% NCA; ASC3 $40K / 3x / 80% NCA; ASC4 $20K / 6x / 30% NCA; ASC5 $25K / 4.5x / 60% NCA; ASC6 $15K / 2x / 85% NCA. Total $155K, blended 3.9x. The CFO wants $25K added with 'maximize new customer count' as the goal. Build the allocation.
answer_criteria: Reframe goal: maximizing NCA count = minimize cost per new customer at the margin. Current ROAS rankings are misleading.
Best current NCA cost: ASC1 ($32), ASC5 ($33), ASC2 ($36), ASC3 ($37), ASC4 ($50), ASC6 ($53).
Estimate marginal NCA cost at +25-50% spend per ASC (typical decay 20-30%): marginal NCA cost rises ~25%. ASC1 marginal: ~$40/NCA; ASC5: ~$41; ASC2: ~$45; ASC3: ~$47; ASC4: ~$63; ASC6: ~$66.
Optimal marginal allocation for NCA count: prioritize ASC1, ASC5, then split ASC2 + ASC3.
Recommended: $10K to ASC1 (lowest marginal NCA cost), $7K to ASC5, $5K to ASC2, $3K to ASC3 = total $25K.
Projected new customer additions: ASC1 +250 NCAs, ASC5 +170, ASC2 +110, ASC3 +65 = ~595 additional NCAs from $25K spend = ~$42 blended marginal NCA cost.
Don't allocate to ASC4 or ASC6: marginal NCA cost too high; if NCA is the goal, these are inefficient at margin.
Stated assumptions: marginal NCA cost decay at 25% per +25% spend; AOV stable; LTV uniform across ASCs.
Implementation: 50% staged increases over 2 weeks; measure marginal NCA at each step; revisit if marginal NCA cost exceeds $60.
Communicate to CFO: '~595 new customers at ~$42 marginal NCA cost — improves total NCA pipeline by 8% while preserving overall account efficiency'.
questions.explanation: NCA-goal allocation requires per-segment NCA-cost computation + marginal-decay modeling. The simplest 'highest ROAS wins' fails the goal completely.

questions.question: A brand spending $100K/month on Meta ran a Conversion Lift study. Test cell (50% of audience): 4,200 conversions. Control cell (50%, no ads): 3,600 conversions. Spend in test: $50K (matched to test-cell exposure). Calculate: incremental conversions, true incremental CPA, true incremental ROAS at $80 AOV, and the gap from Meta's reported metrics (which said 4,200 conversions @ $50K = $11.90 CPA, 6.7x ROAS).
answer_criteria: Incremental conversions = test - control = 4,200 - 3,600 = 600 incremental conversions over the test window.
Incremental CPA = $50K (test spend) / 600 (incremental conversions) = $83.33 incremental CPA. Compare to Meta-reported $11.90 — Meta is over-attributing by ~7x.
Incremental revenue = 600 × $80 AOV = $48,000. Incremental ROAS = $48,000 / $50,000 = 0.96x. Compare to Meta-reported 6.7x — Meta-reported is ~7x the incremental.
Interpretation: Meta is taking credit for 4,200 conversions but only 600 (14%) are incremental. 86% of attributed conversions would have happened without the ads.
Strategic implications: at 0.96x incremental ROAS, the campaign is barely breaking even on a true-causation basis. Margin contribution may be negative.
Diagnose: this very low incrementality (14%) typically indicates a retargeting-heavy audience where most users are already converting. Validate by checking the campaign's audience composition.
If audience is mostly retargeting: this is expected. The strategic action is to redirect spend toward prospecting (where incrementality is much higher, typically 40-70%).
If audience is prospecting: the low incrementality is alarming. Investigate creative, targeting, or audience saturation.
Action 1 — present the lift findings to the team and CFO. Reframe success metric from reported ROAS to incremental ROAS.
Action 2 — recalibrate Smart Bidding targets: Meta's tCPA $11.90 → if true incremental CPA target is $40, set Meta-reported target to ~$5.70 (target × 0.14 = ~14% of $40).
Action 3 — re-design the campaign mix: shift spend from low-incrementality retargeting to higher-incrementality prospecting.
Action 4 — re-run Lift quarterly to measure progress; track incremental ROAS over time as the primary success metric.
questions.explanation: Conversion Lift math translates raw test/control numbers into incremental CPA and iROAS. The reported-vs-incremental gap reveals the true decision-driver.

questions.question: A creative test compared 5 ads in Cell A vs 5 ads in Cell B over 14 days, $5K each. Cell A: 100 conversions. Cell B: 130 conversions. The team wants to know if the difference is statistically significant. Walk through the calculation and recommendation.
answer_criteria: Compute the conversion-rate equivalent: assuming both cells got similar impression / spend, the conversion-count difference reflects underlying CVR difference.
Standard error of conversion-count difference: approximately sqrt(N_A + N_B) = sqrt(100 + 130) = sqrt(230) ≈ 15.
Difference: 130 - 100 = 30 conversions. Z-score = 30/15 = 2.0. Approximate p-value: ~0.046 (one-tailed) or ~0.092 (two-tailed). Borderline significant at one-tailed but not robustly.
Interpretation: directionally Cell B is winning, but the evidence is borderline. With one-tailed test you'd accept; with two-tailed you wouldn't.
Recommendation: extend the test for another 7-14 days to firm up the signal. At ~250 conversions per cell, the same 30% difference becomes solidly significant.
Alternative: pull individual creative performance within each cell. Maybe 1-2 winners in Cell B drove the difference; if so, scale those individual winners regardless of cell-level conclusion.
Consider effect size: 30% conversion volume difference at constant spend = roughly 30% CVR difference, which is meaningful business-wise. Even if statistically borderline, it's worth pursuing.
Decision rule for next time: pre-commit a sample-size target (e.g., 200 conversions per cell minimum) before launching, so the test isn't underpowered.
Action 1 — extend the test 7-14 days for more confidence.
Action 2 — pull creative-level breakdown in parallel to identify specific winners.
Action 3 — set decision rule: 'declare winner if cell-level effect is >20% AND p<0.10 AND best 2 creatives in winning cell outperform best 2 in losing cell'.
Action 4 — document this as a methodology lesson: creative tests need pre-committed sample size.
questions.explanation: Statistical inference on creative tests requires sample-size awareness. Borderline results often demand extension, not premature conclusion.

questions.question: A retargeting campaign serves a 30-day-visitor audience of 400K users. Spend $20K/month delivers 2,000 conversions at $10 CPA, frequency 5.2. The team wants to scale to $50K/month. Quantify the expected outcome.
answer_criteria: At current $20K spend, frequency is 5.2 over 30 days. Audience pool is 400K. Implied avg impressions per user: ~5.2 × (impressions to drive 2,000 conv at $10 CPA) ≈ ~5 impressions per user on average.
Audience capacity check: 2,000 conversions out of 400K reachable users = 0.5% conversion rate. The pool can sustain higher frequency only with diminishing returns.
Scaling to $50K (2.5x spend) on the same 400K pool implies frequency ~13 over 30 days (heavy fatigue territory).

Expected outcomes: (a) frequency surge to 12-13x → CTR drops 30-50%, CPM rises 20-40% from auction pressure on saturated pool; (b) marginal CPA rises to $20-$30 (from $10); (c) total conversions reach maybe 3,000-3,500 (not 5,000 linear-scale).

Net analysis: $50K spend delivering ~3,200 conversions at blended ~$15.60 CPA — versus $20K delivering 2,000 at $10. Marginal contribution: extra $30K spend → +1,200 conversions at $25 marginal CPA. At $90 AOV × 35% margin = $31.50 contribution per conversion. Marginal contribution gain = 1,200 × $31.50 - $30K = $7,800 over 30 days.
Decision: marginally positive but small. Better path: grow the retargetable pool (more prospecting traffic feeding the funnel), THEN scale retargeting proportionally.
Action 1 — DON'T scale retargeting alone to $50K. Pool saturation makes this inefficient.
Action 2 — instead, scale prospecting by $25K to grow the retargetable pool by ~50% over 30 days. Then scale retargeting by $10K-$15K, keeping frequency in the 6-7x range.
Action 3 — build a 'retargeting capacity' model: for every X new prospecting visitors generated, retargeting can absorb $Y additional spend at acceptable frequency.
Action 4 — set the team's expectation: retargeting scales with audience, not budget. Pool growth is the binding constraint.
questions.explanation: Retargeting scales with addressable audience, not budget. The quantitative analysis shows the binding constraint is pool size, not spend capacity.

questions.question: A brand's 12-month historical data shows: each $1 of Meta spend generates $4 in attributed revenue (4x ROAS). MMM-derived incremental ROAS is 2.6x. The CMO is targeting 30% YoY revenue growth, currently 'doing $1.5M from $375K Meta spend'. CFO offers $400K incremental Meta budget. Quantify whether this hits the growth target.
answer_criteria: Current state: $375K spend × 4x ROAS = $1.5M attributed revenue (matches). At 2.6x incremental ROAS, true incremental from Meta = $375K × 2.6 = $975K.
Growth target: $1.5M × 1.30 = $1.95M attributed revenue. Or in incremental terms: need additional $585K incremental revenue (above current $975K incremental from Meta).

Quantify what +$400K Meta spend produces (assuming 80% of average marginal ROAS due to scaling): marginal incremental ROAS ~ 2.6 × 0.80 = 2.08x. $400K × 2.08 = $832K incremental revenue. At 4x reported ROAS × 0.80 = 3.2x marginal reported = $1.28M reported attributed revenue.
Meets the target on attributed metric: $1.5M + $1.28M = $2.78M total. But this overstates because Meta isn't the only channel and growth comes from many sources.

Reframe: if the goal is 30% growth on the $1.5M Meta-attributed revenue specifically, $400K incremental Meta spend producing $1.28M additional attributed revenue overshoots by 4.4x. CFO's offer is more than enough.

If the goal is $1.95M total brand revenue (across all channels) and Meta is currently 30% of brand revenue ($1.5M out of $5M), then Meta needs to produce its share of the $1.5M growth target = ~30% × $1.5M = $450K additional revenue. $400K Meta spend × 3.2x marginal ROAS = $1.28M incremental — easily exceeds.
Recommendation: accept the $400K but stage it (50% increase, then evaluate before adding rest); over-allocation early creates Learning shock.

Alternative recommendation: if attributed-revenue growth is the only goal, $200K incremental Meta spend would suffice. Use the other $200K for diversification (TikTok test, retention email program, etc.).
Stated assumptions: marginal ROAS at 80% of average; LTV stable; no major macro shifts; brand still has unsaturated audience capacity.
Risk callout: high marginal CPA at the new spend level could push contribution into break-even or negative; recommend monthly review.
Communicate to CMO + CFO: 'budget is more than sufficient for 30% growth target; recommend staged deployment + diversification of unallocated portion'.
questions.explanation: Growth-target modeling requires distinguishing attributed vs incremental revenue and modeling marginal performance. The quantitative reasoning often reveals over-budgeting or misaligned targets.

questions.question: A campaign on Highest Value reports: $80K spend, 1,200 conversions, $300K revenue (3.75x ROAS), AOV $250. The CFO wants to know: what's the ratio of high-value to low-value conversions, and is Highest Value 'finding' more value or just sorting existing demand?
answer_criteria: Pull the AOV distribution histogram. Compute: median AOV vs mean AOV. If median is $200 and mean is $250, the distribution has high-value outliers pulling the average up.
Segment by AOV quintile: top 20% (highest AOV), middle 60%, bottom 20%. Compute: what % of conversions are in each, and what % of revenue.

Typical distribution for Highest Value campaigns: top 20% of conversions drive 35-50% of revenue. Bottom 20% drive 5-10% of revenue. Highest Value's algorithm preferentially serves to predicted-high-value users.

Compare to brand baseline: if organic / non-Highest-Value campaigns show median AOV of $180 (vs Highest Value's $250), Highest Value IS shifting the mix toward higher-value buyers — but is this real value creation or sorting?

Sorting test: compare Highest Value's high-value-buyer cohort to organic high-value-buyer cohort. If they're the same buyers (high-LTV-segment users who would have bought premium anyway), it's sorting. If Highest Value is acquiring high-value buyers organic acquisition wouldn't have reached, it's value creation.

Run an incrementality test: 30-day Conversion Lift on Highest Value campaign. If lift is uniform across AOV quintiles, sorting effect is real but value-create may also be real. If lift is concentrated in top quintile, Highest Value is selecting from existing demand, not creating it.
Action 1 — pull the AOV distribution and quintile analysis (this is the load-bearing data).
Action 2 — run a Lift study to differentiate sorting from value creation.
Action 3 — if sorting: Highest Value is essentially overpaying for users who would have bought premium anyway. Reduce spend; the contribution per dollar is lower than reported.
Action 4 — if value creation: Highest Value is genuinely acquiring incremental high-LTV customers. Scale within marginal-ROAS limits.
Action 5 — communicate to CFO: 'AOV lift is real on the metric; the strategic question is whether Highest Value is creating or sorting value; running a Lift study to find out'.
Long-term: re-run quarterly. Sorting tendency tends to grow over time as the algorithm exhausts the premium-buyer pool.
questions.explanation: Highest Value's AOV lift is often a sorting effect, not value creation. The quantitative analysis distinguishes the two via distribution analysis + incrementality testing.

questions.question: A team running 8 prospecting ad sets in 1 campaign (CBO) sees the algorithm allocate 70% of spend to 2 ad sets and 5% each to the other 6. The other 6 are starving. Quantitative analysis: should they restructure?
answer_criteria: Assess: is the 70/30 allocation reflecting real performance differences or algorithmic over-confidence?
Pull per-ad-set performance: ROAS, CPA, NCA rate, conversion volume. The 2 over-served ad sets should have meaningfully better metrics if the allocation is justified.
If the over-served ad sets ARE genuinely outperforming (e.g., 4.5x ROAS vs 2.5x for the others): the algorithm is doing its job. The starved ad sets are correctly being de-prioritized.

If the starved ad sets show similar performance per the small sample they did get: the algorithm may have over-concentrated based on early signal noise. Restructure into separate campaigns to give each a fair shot.

Quantify the cost of starvation: 6 ad sets at 5% spend each = total $300/day if campaign is $10K/day. At $50/conv across 6 ad sets = ~6 total conversions/day from starved ad sets. The algorithm thinks these are low-value; but if performance per-conv is reasonable, additional learning data could improve them.

Hybrid strategy: keep top 2 as a CBO campaign (algorithm allocates between similar-economics ad sets); separate the other 6 into ABO (manual budget) where each gets a fair $1K-$2K budget for 4 weeks to gather evidence.
After 4 weeks of forced equal spend, decide: ad sets that proved their performance graduate to a CBO campaign; ad sets that confirmed weakness get killed.
Stated assumptions: starved ad sets aren't structurally different (e.g., new Learning Phase) from over-served; current 70/30 may be early-signal artifact.
Action 1 — pull per-ad-set performance over 30 days; look for evidence of real performance differentiation vs. algorithmic concentration.
Action 2 — implement the hybrid: CBO for proven winners, ABO for unproven candidates with forced equal spend.
Action 3 — re-evaluate at week 4; consolidate validated performers, kill unvalidated ones.
Action 4 — long-term: when launching new ad sets in CBO, give them 1-2 weeks of forced budget to overcome the algorithm's cold-start bias.
questions.explanation: CBO concentration can be justified algorithmic preference OR early-signal over-confidence. The quantitative analysis distinguishes the two and prescribes a forced-spend test for unproven ad sets.

questions.question: A creative testing program ran 60 ads over 90 days. Results: 12 ads (20%) are 'evergreen' (sustained performance >3 months); 24 ads (40%) were 'one-hit wonders' (won 7-14 days then faded); 24 ads (40%) failed early (<7 days). The team thinks: 'Evergreen rate is 20%, that's our success metric.' Build a more rigorous quantitative framework.
answer_criteria: 20% evergreen rate is one metric, but it doesn't capture the full economic value of the testing program.
Compute total value generated: sum spend on each creative × ROAS during its active life. Compare to the cost of the testing program (creative production cost + algorithmic learning cost).

Decompose value: evergreen creatives often generate 60-80% of total spend × ROAS contribution. 'One-hit wonders' contribute meaningful short-term value before fading. Failures are the cost of discovery.

Compute economic ROI of the program: (total contribution from all 60 creatives - cost to produce 60 creatives) / cost. If positive, the program is creating value. If negative, the program is over-investing in testing.

Reframe success metrics: (a) total contribution per testing dollar (the program's economic ROI); (b) evergreen rate (downstream sustainability); (c) lifetime spend per evergreen winner (capacity to scale); (d) hypothesis validation rate (% of tests that confirmed/refuted a stated hypothesis).
Identify creative-pattern winners: among the 12 evergreen ads, what's common? Format? Hook style? Message? Use this to refine future briefs.
Compute hypothesis hit rate: did each of the 60 tests have a stated hypothesis pre-launch? If yes, what % validated? If no, the program is exploring, not learning — improve brief discipline.
Action 1 — build the value-decomposition dashboard: contribution per creative × creative lifetime; identify the highest-value creatives to model future briefs after.
Action 2 — measure economic ROI of the testing program: should the company invest more or less in creative testing?
Action 3 — institute hypothesis-driven briefs: every test states a specific hypothesis. Track validation rate as a key metric.
Action 4 — redesign success metrics to capture multi-dimensional value: not just evergreen rate.
Action 5 — quarterly review: which creative themes / formats compounded? What's the next quarter's bet portfolio?
Communicate to leadership: 'evergreen rate is one of four success metrics; our program's economic ROI is X, with insights informing next quarter's creative strategy'.
questions.explanation: Single-metric evaluation of creative-testing programs misses the economic and learning value. Multi-dimensional measurement reveals true program ROI.

questions.question: A team has 3 LAL audiences: 1% LAL ($15K spend, 4.5x ROAS, audience size 3M), 3% LAL ($25K, 3.8x, 9M), 5% LAL ($30K, 3.0x, 15M). The team proposes consolidating into a 'stacked LAL 1-5%' audience. Quantitative analysis: is consolidation likely to help or hurt?
answer_criteria: Audience size at 1-5% stacked: roughly 15M (the 5% LAL is largely a superset of 1% and 3%). Slight de-duplication of overlapping users.

ROAS prediction for stacked: depends on how Meta's algorithm allocates within the consolidated audience. With Smart Bidding, the algorithm should serve preferentially to predicted-high-converting users (which would be 1% LAL similar). Expected blended: 3.5-4.0x.
Comparison to current performance: total current spend $70K at blended 3.7x ROAS. Stacked LAL with $70K spend likely delivers 3.5-3.8x — slightly worse to similar.
What's lost: ability to control budget split between tiers. If 1% is the highest-quality, current architecture allocates 21% of spend to 1% explicitly; stacked architecture lets the algorithm decide.
What's gained: simpler management, fewer ad sets to maintain, less risk of audience-overlap inefficiency between separate ad sets.

Real risk: if the 1% LAL has been running well at $15K but the algorithm in stacked mode allocates only 5% (~$3.5K) to 1%-similar users, you lose the high-quality concentration. The blended ROAS may suffer.
Hypothesis-driven approach: this is a test, not an obvious win. Run for 4 weeks, measure: (a) blended ROAS, (b) total conversions, (c) NCA rate, (d) ROAS drift over time.
Decision rule: consolidate if blended ROAS is within 5% of current AND NCA rate is preserved AND total volume holds.
Stated assumptions: Meta's algorithm in stacked mode allocates rationally to 1%-tier users; audience overlap doesn't create cannibalization.
Recommendation: don't consolidate based on intuition. Run a 50/50 split test (50% spend on stacked LAL, 50% on current segmented structure) for 4 weeks; measure outcomes.
Action 1 — design the test rigorously (Meta Experiments tool to prevent auction overlap).
Action 2 — pre-commit decision rule before launch.
Action 3 — measure NCA, AOV, and reactive metrics, not just ROAS.
Action 4 — execute decision after 4 weeks based on data, not management preference.
questions.explanation: Consolidation decisions depend on whether the algorithm can replicate the human-architected differentiation. Quantitative analysis frames it as a test, not a default.

questions.question: A brand at $150K/month Meta spend has these 90-day metrics: Reported ROAS 4.2x, Shopify last-click ROAS 1.8x, GA4 ROAS 2.1x, MMM-derived iROAS 2.8x, Conversion Lift iROAS 2.2x. Build a quantitative reconciliation explaining the gaps and recommending which to use for budget decisions.
answer_criteria: Reconcile each measurement: Reported (4.2x): includes view-through, dedup-imperfect, 7-day-click + 1-day-view window. Shopify Last-Click (1.8x): UTM-only, last-click. GA4 (2.1x): last-non-direct-click, view-blind. MMM (2.8x): full Meta-attributable contribution incl. brand-search lift. Lift (2.2x): direct test/control measurement of incremental.

Decompose the gaps: Reported vs Shopify gap (4.2 → 1.8) is mostly view-through (~1.0x of inflation) + multi-touch lost in Last-Click (~0.5x). Shopify vs GA4 gap (1.8 → 2.1) is GA4's better cross-session attribution. Reported vs MMM gap (4.2 → 2.8) is the non-incremental conversions Meta claims credit for. Reported vs Lift gap (4.2 → 2.2) is similar but slightly more conservative.
Build the framework: there is no 'true' single number. Different decisions need different measurements.
For Smart Bidding tCPA target: use Reported, since that's what Smart Bidding optimizes against. Translate desired incremental CPA to reported CPA via the Reported/Lift ratio (4.2/2.2 = 1.9x).
For finance reporting: use Shopify last-click as the conservative book-of-record (auditable, source-of-truth). Lift study findings provide the incrementality context.
For budget allocation across channels: use MMM-derived 2.8x as the comparable across channels. Lift's 2.2x is more conservative.
For campaign-level optimization (which campaigns to scale/cut): use the Reported metric for relative comparison across Meta campaigns (since they all suffer the same biases).
Stated assumptions: Lift is the gold standard for short-term incrementality; MMM captures longer-term effects; reporting biases are stable over time.
Risk callout: re-run Lift quarterly. If the Reported/Lift ratio shifts dramatically, methodology needs investigation.
Action 1 — document this framework as the brand's measurement playbook; specify which metric drives which decision.
Action 2 — train the team on the framework so future analysts use the right metric for the right decision.
Action 3 — build a dashboard showing all 5 metrics side by side, with the use-case for each clearly labeled.
Communicate to CMO: 'these aren't competing — they answer different questions. Decision-by-metric mapping below.'
questions.explanation: Multi-measurement reconciliation is a literacy problem. The quantitative framework maps each metric to the decision it serves, rather than picking one as truth.

questions.question: Design an experiment to test whether 9-second video creative outperforms 30-second video creative for Meta prospecting on Reels placement. Brand: skincare DTC. Budget: $80K test over 30 days. State all methodology elements.
answer_criteria: Pre-commit primary metric: 7-day-click + 1-day-view ROAS for prospecting on Reels placement.
Pre-commit secondary metrics: hook rate (3-sec view rate), CPM, CTR, AOV, NCA rate. State which would influence the decision if primary is inconclusive.
Define minimum detectable effect: e.g., 'we want to detect a 15% ROAS difference with 80% statistical power'. This drives sample sizing.
Use Meta Experiments tool (NOT manual ad-set splits). Experiments tool does user-level randomization, prevents auction overlap.

Test arms: Cell A — 6 ads, all 9-second video, native vertical. Cell B — 6 ads, all 30-second video, native vertical. Equal asset count, equal aspect ratio, identical creative concepts (same hook, same product), only duration varies.
Force placement: Reels-only delivery to isolate the test effect. Disable Feed/Stories/in-stream to prevent placement confound.
Spend allocation: $40K per cell. At industry-typical conversion volume for skincare DTC at this spend, expect ~600-900 conversions per cell — sufficient for ~15% ROAS difference detection.
Pre-launch checklist: identical landing pages, identical pixel/CAPI, identical audience (broad prospecting), identical bid strategy.

Pre-register the analysis plan: primary + secondary metrics; exclusion of first 3 days (Learning); decision rule (e.g., 'ship the winner if primary ROAS difference >12% with p<0.10 AND directionally consistent on hook rate').
What NOT to do: don't read results before day 14; don't add new creatives mid-test (poisons the test); don't optimize one cell more than the other (sandbagging); don't change spend mid-test.

Decision framework after results: if 9s wins decisively → standardize on 9s for Reels; if 30s wins → invest in production capacity for longer-form Reels content; if inconclusive → hypothesis didn't differentiate, design a follow-up test with bigger expected effect (e.g., 6s vs 30s).
Document for archive: hypothesis, design, results, learning. Future creative briefs reference this.
Communicate to creative team: this is one test about one variable. Don't generalize to all video, all placements.
questions.explanation: Rigorous creative experiments require pre-committed metrics, proper randomization, explicit power analysis, and a written analysis plan. The most common failure modes — auction overlap, p-hacking, post-hoc metric shopping — are anticipated and prevented.

questions.question: Design a 'creative iteration system' for a DTC brand running 30+ ads/month. The brand currently has no system — creative gets briefed ad-hoc, results aren't archived, learnings don't compound. Outline the system structure.
answer_criteria: Hypothesis framework: every new creative starts with a stated hypothesis in one of four categories: (a) hook variation, (b) value-prop variation, (c) format variation, (d) audience-fit variation. No more than one variable per concept lineage.
Brief template: each creative brief includes — hypothesis, expected learning, primary metric, reference comparable, kill threshold, brief author.
Test cadence: weekly creative drops (6-8 new ads/week) into a dedicated 'creative-testing' ASC with a defined budget (~10-15% of total).
Decision rules — kill: after $X spend with no spend share earned by algorithm (X = ~5x typical CPA, e.g., $200 if average CPA is $40). Kill: clear underperformance (CPA >2x target) at >$X spend.
Decision rules — iterate: 70-110% of benchmark = kill but spawn 1-2 variants in next sprint. Decision rules — scale: >110% of benchmark sustained 14+ days = move to evergreen pool.

Learning archive: every test logged with hypothesis + asset + result + tagged learnings (e.g., 'UGC outperforms studio for hook rate in this category'; 'shorter hook performs better for younger demographic'). Searchable.
Concept lineage tracking: each successful concept spawns 3-5 variants in next sprint, each with one variable change. Track 'creative trees'.

Avoid common pitfalls: don't kill too fast (algorithm needs ~50 link clicks before signal stabilizes); don't iterate on losers (instead spawn new hypotheses); don't run too many cells simultaneously (auction overlap).
Measurement infra: weekly dashboard showing hook rate, hold rate, CTR, ROAS, algorithmic spend share per creative.
Quarterly retrospective: meta-level review — which hypotheses validated, which formats compounded, what's next quarter's bet portfolio?
Single ownership: assign one creative-strategist as the system owner. Without ownership the program decays into ad-hoc requests within 60 days.
Brief-to-launch SLA: target 5-7 day brief-to-launch cycle. Bottleneck identification: which step adds most lag? Production? Approval? Asset build?
Document the SOP and onboard the team. Make it a system, not a person.
questions.explanation: Sustainable creative iteration is systems thinking — hypothesis-driven, cadenced, decision-ruled, archived, and owned. Pattern-matching answers list 'test more creative'; reasoning answers describe a system.

questions.question: A DTC brand wants to know: 'Does our Meta ASC genuinely create incremental new customers, or does it cannibalize organic / email-driven sales?' Design the experiment.
answer_criteria: Pre-state the precise question: 'How many of the new-customer purchases attributed to ASC would NOT have happened if ASC were paused?' This is an incrementality question, not an attribution question.
