Action 2 — audit asset groups and feed for completeness/approval.
Action 3 — if running tROAS, switch to Maximize Conversion Value (no target) for the first 4 weeks of Learning.
Action 4 — add audience signals if missing (customer list, in-market segments, custom intent).
Action 5 — verify the campaign isn't accidentally restricted to obscure placements/languages/geos.

Action 6 — if all the above are correct: PMax may need 4-6 weeks for cold accounts. $20K in 14 days is reasonable spend; lack of conversions may indicate a creative or targeting issue rather than time.
Action 7 — counter-recommend: don't kill the campaign yet; fix the underlying issue and give it another 14 days. Killing forfeits Learning.
Communicate: 'identifying root cause; fix-then-wait approach is right path; will update in 48h with diagnostic + action plan'.
questions.explanation: PMax 0-conversion failures usually trace to tracking, feed, asset groups, or aggressive bid targets. The diagnostic walks the priority order systematically.

questions.question: After switching to DDA from Last Click 30 days ago, brand campaign reported conversions dropped 22% while non-brand reported conversions rose 15%. CFO is confused about whether performance changed. Walk through the explanation.
answer_criteria: Reframe: total reported conversions changed by less than the per-campaign shifts suggest. The change is attribution methodology, not actual performance.
DDA mechanism: distributes credit across the journey rather than 100% to last touch. Brand campaigns (typically last-touch winners under Last Click) lose share to non-brand assists.
Quantify the redistribution: 22% drop in brand + 15% rise in non-brand = net likely close to zero on total conversions (the redistribution is largely zero-sum).

Smart Bidding implications: tCPA / tROAS targets may need recalibration since Smart Bidding optimizes against the new attribution model. Brand may now appear less efficient (and the algorithm may bid less); non-brand may appear more efficient (and algorithm may bid more).

Verify: pull total Meta-attributed conversions across all campaigns over the 30-day period. If total conversions are within 5% of pre-switch baseline, performance hasn't changed; only credit distribution.
Verify: pull source-of-truth metrics (Shopify, GA4) for total revenue. If those are stable, performance is unchanged.
Action 1 — explain to CFO: 'this is attribution method change, not real performance change. Total conversions across all campaigns is essentially the same; credit moved between brand and non-brand.'
Action 2 — recalibrate Smart Bidding targets: brand's tCPA may need slight loosening (it now appears less efficient); non-brand may need slight tightening (now appears more efficient).
Action 3 — set a 30-day reset period: don't make budget allocation decisions based on the new attribution until 30 days of data has accumulated under DDA. Early decisions risk over-correction.

Action 4 — communicate the framework to leadership: DDA is a more accurate distribution of credit (Google's preferred + recommended), but it's still attribution, not incremental contribution. For incrementality, run Lift studies.
Action 5 — schedule a 60-day review: after the dust settles, re-evaluate budget allocation using the new attribution + any Lift study findings.
questions.explanation: DDA's credit redistribution looks like performance changes but isn't. The diagnostic separates attribution-shift from real-change and recalibrates Smart Bidding accordingly.

questions.question: A tROAS 5x campaign is showing erratic 7-day performance: 6.2x, 3.8x, 5.5x, 4.1x, 5.8x for 5 consecutive weeks. The team wants to know if Smart Bidding is broken. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: ±25% weekly variance is moderately high but may reflect sample noise + auction dynamics. Diagnose vs investigate.
Compute conversion volume per week: at 100 conv/week, the standard error of weekly ROAS is ~10-15%. The observed variance of ~25% is roughly 2x noise — outside pure sampling noise but not extreme.
Hypothesis 1: low conversion volume amplifies week-over-week swings. Pull weekly conversion count; if <30/week, the variance is mostly noise.
Hypothesis 2: weekly seasonality — different mix of users converting on different days. Pull day-of-week breakdown.
Hypothesis 3: external auction events (competitor launches, market events) cause weekly fluctuations. Check Auction Insights for competitor activity changes.
Hypothesis 4: creative or audience changes mid-period — even unintentional. Audit the change log for the 5-week period.
Aggregate analysis: compute the 5-week average. (6.2+3.8+5.5+4.1+5.8)/5 = 5.08x — exactly at target. The mean is fine; the variance is the question.
Action 1 — verify 5-week mean is at target (it is). The campaign IS hitting target on average.
Action 2 — pull weekly conversion count to assess sample-size contribution to variance.
Action 3 — pull weekly auction insights to look for competitor pressure spikes coinciding with low-ROAS weeks.
Action 4 — if variance is mostly sample noise: the campaign is fine; reframe expectations from week-over-week to 4-week-rolling.
Action 5 — if variance is competitive / external: it's a market characteristic, not a campaign bug.
Action 6 — DO NOT make Smart Bidding changes based on weekly volatility — would create more harm via Learning resets.
Communicate: 'weekly variance is noisy but mean is at target; switching to 4-week-rolling reporting; algorithm is performing correctly.'
questions.explanation: Smart Bidding produces noisy short-window metrics. Aggregating to 4-week-rolling reveals the true performance picture; reactive adjustments to weekly noise create Learning damage.

questions.question: A retailer's Merchant Center shows 12% of products disapproved. Their PMax / Shopping campaigns are missing 'a lot of inventory' (per the team's intuition). Walk through the diagnostic.
answer_criteria: 12% disapproval rate is meaningful but the impact depends on which products are disapproved. Walk through the priority analysis.

Pull disapproval reasons by category in Merchant Center. Common categories: pricing mismatch (LP price ≠ feed price), missing GTIN, image quality, restricted product (regulated category), policy violation.

Compute: which products are disapproved by impression share or revenue contribution? If disapproved items are top 20% of revenue contributors, the impact is severe. If they're long-tail, less critical.

Diagnose by reason: pricing mismatches usually mean the LP price changed but feed didn't update. Feed-LP sync issue. Missing GTIN often means new products without proper data entry. Image quality may be platform-policy enforcement.
Action 1 — fix pricing mismatches first (typically the biggest revenue impact + easiest to fix). Verify feed updates within X hours of LP price changes.
Action 2 — fix missing GTINs by adding from product catalog or marking 'identifier exists: false' for own-brand items.
Action 3 — fix image quality (white background, no text overlay, correct resolution).
Action 4 — for restricted categories (regulated products), check if Merchant Center policies allow your category; some require special approval.
Action 5 — set up automated daily monitoring of disapproval rate; alert if disapproval rate exceeds 5%.
Action 6 — measure revenue impact: pull projected revenue if all disapprovals were fixed. If projected revenue impact is >10% of current revenue, prioritize urgent fix.
Long-term: weekly Merchant Center hygiene review. Disapprovals accumulate when ignored; they're easy to prevent with regular maintenance.
Communicate: 'disapproval impact is X% of revenue contribution; fixing top-3 disapproval reasons in next 14 days for Y% recovery'.
questions.explanation: Feed disapprovals require systematic diagnosis by reason category and revenue impact. Bulk fixing in priority order (pricing → GTIN → images) usually recovers most lost inventory.

questions.question: A B2B SaaS team's Google Ads account has 8 Search campaigns. Total spend $40K/month. They notice: per-campaign Quality Scores range 4-9. Average account Quality Score: 6.5. Some keywords have QS 3. Walk through the diagnostic and prescription.
answer_criteria: Account-wide QS 6.5 is mediocre; QS 3 keywords are dragging account performance. Diagnose what's failing.
Pull QS components per low-QS keyword: (a) Expected CTR, (b) Ad Relevance, (c) Landing Page Experience. Each can be 'Below Average', 'Average', 'Above Average'.

Common failure mode: 'Ad Relevance: Below Average' means the ad copy doesn't include the keyword or doesn't match its intent. Common in accounts with broad ad-group themes where one ad serves many keywords.
Common failure mode: 'Expected CTR: Below Average' means historically the ad gets lower CTR than competitors at similar position. Indicates copy needs refresh.
Common failure mode: 'Landing Page Experience: Below Average' means LP doesn't match the keyword intent, loads slow, or has weak content. May require CRO investment.
Action 1 — diagnose by component. Each component has different remedies.
Action 2 — for Ad Relevance issues: tighten ad-group themes (move keywords with mismatched RSA to new ad groups with relevant copy). Update RSAs to include keyword in headlines.
Action 3 — for Expected CTR issues: refresh ad copy with more compelling angles. Test new headlines against current.
Action 4 — for Landing Page Experience issues: invest in LP optimization — improve relevance to keyword, reduce load time (target <3s), improve mobile-friendliness.
Action 5 — at the campaign level: pull QS distribution. If a single campaign has all the QS-3 keywords, focus there.
Action 6 — quantify the cost: low QS = high CPC. Improving QS 3→7 typically reduces CPC by 40-60%, which directly improves account-level efficiency.
Action 7 — set a 60-day improvement target. Track QS trends weekly. Target: account QS 7+, no keyword <5.
Long-term: include QS health checks in monthly review; QS degradation is a leading indicator of campaign health issues.
Communicate: 'QS isn't vanity; improving QS from 6.5 to 7.5 across the account would reduce CPC by ~25%, freeing budget for additional volume'.
questions.explanation: Quality Score diagnosis requires component-level analysis (CTR, Relevance, LP). Each component has specific remedies. Account-level improvements compound into significant CPC savings.

questions.question: A team's Maximize Conversion Value campaign was tracking $200K monthly revenue at 5x ROAS. They set a tROAS target of 5x last month to 'lock in' performance. Since then: revenue dropped to $140K (3.5x), conversion volume down 35%. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: Setting tROAS = current ROAS is a common mistake. The current ROAS reflects the algorithm's optimization at no constraint. Adding a target floor at the same level constrains the algorithm.
Mechanism: when no tROAS was set (Maximize Conversion Value), the algorithm took every auction it could win at any predicted ROAS. The 5x average reflected the natural mix.

With tROAS 5x set, the algorithm now filters out auctions where predicted ROAS is below 5x. Many of those auctions were profitable in aggregate (some below 5x, some above); filtering them loses conversion volume + total revenue.
Result: smaller conversion pool → smaller revenue → ROAS may rise to ~5x but absolute revenue and conversion volume drop.
Action 1 — diagnose: pull the ROAS distribution of conversions pre-tROAS-setting. If many conversions were below 5x ROAS, setting target at 5x filters them out.
Action 2 — recommend: lower tROAS to ~80% of natural performance (e.g., 4.0x if natural is 5x) to capture more auctions while still maintaining a floor. This balances volume + ROAS.
Action 3 — alternative: revert to Maximize Conversion Value (no target) to recover full conversion volume. Accept the natural ROAS distribution.
Action 4 — revisit the target: if 5x is a hard business requirement (e.g., margin floor), accept the lower volume; if 5x was aspirational, lower it.
Action 5 — wait 7-10 days after the change for Smart Bidding to relearn under the new constraint.

Action 6 — long-term: tROAS targets should be set 10-20% below natural performance to capture more volume; set above natural performance to constrain heavily for margin protection (with awareness of volume cost).
Communicate to team: 'tROAS at current natural performance level always loses volume; the right calibration is below natural for growth, above for margin protection'.
questions.explanation: Setting tROAS at current natural performance constrains the algorithm and loses volume. Targets should be calibrated for desired tradeoff, not 'lock in'.

questions.question: A PMax campaign for a retailer has $50K spend, 4x ROAS. The team notices the 'Insights' tab shows 35% of spend going to 'YouTube' placements with very low conversion attribution from YouTube. The ad ops team thinks YouTube is wasted budget and proposes restricting PMax to Shopping-only via 'feed-only' setup. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: YouTube spend with low last-click attribution is suspicious but not necessarily 'wasted'. YouTube primarily drives upper-funnel awareness; conversions credit downstream channels.
Verify upper-funnel signal: did brand search volume rise during periods of high YouTube spend? Pull Search Console / Google Trends for brand queries.
Verify cross-channel halo: did organic traffic / direct traffic increase? GA4 should show whether YouTube exposure correlates with later session sources.
Hypothesis 1: YouTube serves as awareness; conversion credit goes to brand-search or direct. Killing YouTube would reduce upper-funnel demand creation, downstream affecting brand search volume.
Hypothesis 2: YouTube is genuinely wasted (placements showing to wrong audience, low-quality inventory). PMax's algorithm may be filling budget on cheap inventory regardless of value.
Verify: pull YouTube placement-level detail (where possible in PMax Insights). Are placements skewed toward content unrelated to the brand's audience? If yes, hypothesis 2 is more likely.

Verify: run an A/B test where possible. Pause PMax in 50% of geos for 14-21 days. Measure brand search volume + conversion volume in pause vs running geos. If YouTube is driving downstream value, paused geos will see decline beyond PMax's reported attribution.
Action 1 — gather data on brand search lift, direct traffic, organic-to-checkout journeys.
Action 2 — run the geo holdout test before committing to feed-only.
Action 3 — if YouTube is driving downstream value: keep PMax with full inventory; recognize the YouTube spend is awareness, not direct response. Restructure measurement to credit it.
Action 4 — if YouTube is wasted: switch to feed-only PMax for direct-response, run a separate Demand Gen / YouTube campaign for awareness with awareness-appropriate metrics.
Action 5 — if the holdout test is inconclusive: maintain status quo; the cost of being wrong (killing valuable awareness) exceeds the cost of waste.
Communicate: 'YouTube placement value requires holdout testing to confirm; killing without testing risks demand-creation loss'.
questions.explanation: YouTube spend in PMax requires holdout testing to distinguish awareness value from waste. The diagnostic prevents premature elimination based on last-click attribution.

questions.question: A non-brand Search campaign post-Phrase-Match-update sees the Search Terms Report flooded with off-topic queries (e.g., 'free [product]', 'how to [DIY alternative]', 'jobs at [competitor]'). CTR has dropped 25% over 60 days. Diagnose and prescribe.
answer_criteria: Phrase Match's expanded behavior post-2021 (incorporating BMM) catches more queries than expected. Without aggressive negative-keyword hygiene, off-topic queries flood in.
Off-topic queries cost: low CTR (drags Quality Score), wasted spend (irrelevant clicks), distorted Smart Bidding signal (the algorithm learns from off-topic conversion patterns).

Action 1 — pull the Search Terms Report; categorize off-topic queries by pattern. Common categories: free / DIY (intent mismatch), competitor names (intent mismatch), job-related (intent mismatch), education / how-to (intent mismatch).
Action 2 — add Negative Keywords for each pattern. For 'free' / 'DIY' / 'jobs' / 'careers' / 'how to' — phrase or broad negative match. Aggressive at first; refine if blocking valid traffic.
Action 3 — use Negative Keyword Lists in Shared Library so the same negatives apply across all relevant campaigns.
Action 4 — for systemic patterns: if 'how to' queries are flooding in, the campaign may be triggering on tutorial intent. May require keyword-match-type tightening (Phrase → Exact for top keywords).

Action 5 — re-evaluate keyword themes: are the underlying keywords too broad? E.g., 'lawn mowing' is broad enough to trigger 'how to mow a lawn' tutorials. Consider narrower themes ('professional lawn care service').
Action 6 — measure impact: track CTR + irrelevant-query share weekly. Target: 80%+ of queries should be on-topic.
Action 7 — long-term: weekly Search Terms hygiene as a standard process. Negatives accumulate; un-managed accounts drift toward irrelevant traffic.
Action 8 — re-evaluate Quality Score after 30 days of cleanup; expect QS to recover as CTR improves on more-relevant queries.
Communicate: 'this is a standard post-Phrase-update issue; aggressive negative hygiene + weekly review will restore performance over 30-60 days'.
questions.explanation: Phrase Match's 2021 behavior change requires more aggressive negative-keyword hygiene. The diagnostic categorizes off-topic queries and prescribes systematic cleanup.

questions.question: A team running tCPA $80 has stable performance for 4 months. They added 'Conversion Value Rules' last month to weight enterprise leads at 3x non-enterprise. Now: conversion volume up 5%, CPA up 18% (to $94), but reported revenue up 35%. The team is confused. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: Conversion Value Rules weight conversion VALUE in Smart Bidding; the algorithm now optimizes against weighted value, not raw conversion count.

Mechanism: with 3x weight on enterprise leads, the algorithm bids more aggressively on auctions predicted to generate enterprise leads (which may have higher CPC + lower CVR but higher value). This raises CPA but raises value-per-conversion more.
Verify: pull conversion-mix data — what % of conversions are enterprise (now vs pre-Value-Rules)? If enterprise % rose from 20% to 35%, the algorithm is shifting toward higher-value conversions.
Verify: revenue per conversion = $94 CPA × overall ROAS. If revenue per conversion rose more than CPA, the rules are working as intended (higher-value conversions worth more).

Hypothesis: the team is comparing CPA without context. CPA (raw cost per conversion) rises when the algorithm targets higher-value conversions; the right metric is now value-per-conversion or weighted-CPA.
Action 1 — explain to team: Conversion Value Rules CHANGE the optimization target. CPA is no longer the right success metric; value (revenue, weighted conversion value) is.
Action 2 — verify the weighted CPA is still acceptable: enterprise-weighted CPA = (CPA / value-weighted-conversions). If this is improved vs pre-rules, the rules are working.
Action 3 — pull the metric the rules optimize for: total weighted conversion value / spend. Should be improved.
Action 4 — set the team's expectation: with Value Rules enabled, CPA is a lagging / partial metric. Track weighted-value-per-spend instead.
Action 5 — quarterly review: are the rule weights producing the right business outcome? If enterprise leads aren't actually 3x more valuable downstream, the rule weight may need adjustment.
Action 6 — communicate to leadership: 'Conversion Value Rules are working — algorithm is finding more valuable leads; reported revenue up 35% means the rule weights are being honored'.
questions.explanation: Conversion Value Rules change Smart Bidding's optimization target. CPA becomes misleading; value-weighted metrics are the right measure.

questions.question: A retailer with 5,000 SKUs sees PMax allocating 70% of Shopping spend to just 80 SKUs. The other 4,920 are getting minimal exposure. The team thinks the algorithm is 'cherry-picking'. Walk through the diagnostic.
answer_criteria: 80 / 5,000 SKUs getting 70% of spend = highly concentrated allocation. May reflect Pareto distribution (real demand pattern) or algorithmic over-confidence.

Verify: pull search-volume / product-demand data per SKU (e.g., from Google Trends or internal search-query data). If the 80 SKUs are also the highest-demand products, the allocation reflects market reality, not algorithmic bias.
Verify: pull conversion-rate per SKU. The 80 may have meaningfully higher CVR (best-sellers, optimized titles, top reviews) — the algorithm's preference is justified.
Verify: pull margin per SKU. If the 80 are highest-margin AND have the right Performance Max value-bidding setup, the algorithm is correctly finding margin-maximizing inventory.
Hypothesis 1: the algorithm is correctly serving the 80 SKUs with most market demand. Underspend on 4,920 reflects their actual lack of demand.
Hypothesis 2: feed quality issues on the 4,920 (poor titles, missing GTIN, low image quality) prevent them from competing in auctions. Improving feed quality would unlock them.
Hypothesis 3: the algorithm has over-converged on early-winning SKUs and is missing emerging opportunities in the long tail.
Action 1 — pull the feed-quality scores for the underspent SKUs. If most have feed issues, fixing those would unlock additional inventory.
Action 2 — pull search-volume for underspent SKUs. If they have meaningful search volume + good feed quality but no PMax exposure, the algorithm may be over-converged.

Action 3 — segment PMax campaigns: create a separate PMax for the long-tail 4,920 with a dedicated budget so they're not competing for share with the 80 stars. Often called 'low-volume PMax' segmentation.
Action 4 — for individual high-priority SKUs that PMax is starving: run a parallel Standard Shopping campaign on those specific products with manual bidding to ensure visibility.
Action 5 — invest in feed-quality cleanup for the long-tail; titles and images are the highest-leverage interventions.
Action 6 — measure: do the long-tail SKUs gain share over 30-60 days post-intervention? If yes, the diagnosis was correct.
Long-term: monthly review of SKU-level spend distribution; rebalance if structural over-concentration develops.
questions.explanation: PMax SKU concentration may reflect real market dynamics or algorithmic over-convergence. The diagnostic separates demand-driven from algorithm-driven concentration.

questions.question: A team's GA4 shows Google Ads 'Paid Search' driving 30% of total revenue. Google Ads UI shows 38% of revenue attributed to Google. The 8-percentage-point gap has been consistent for 6 months. Diagnose what explains it and which is 'correct'.
answer_criteria: Both numbers can be 'right' — they measure different things. Diagnose the methodological difference.
Google Ads UI uses DDA (Data-Driven Attribution); GA4 uses last-non-direct-click by default (or DDA if configured). Different attribution models = different credit distribution.
DDA gives more credit to upper-funnel paid touches that GA4's last-click partly under-credits. The 8-pp gap is often DDA's redistribution captured in Google Ads but not in GA4.
Cross-device: Google Ads tracks cross-device journeys for signed-in users; GA4 may not capture these consistently. Cross-device adds incremental Google Ads attribution.
View-through: Google Ads counts engaged-view conversions on YouTube + Display; GA4 may not. View-through credit narrows the gap.
Model both attributions in GA4: if you switch GA4 to DDA, the gap closes. If you compare Google Ads (DDA) vs GA4 (DDA), they should be much closer (within 2-3 pp).
Hypothesis: the 8-pp gap is mostly attribution methodology, not data error. Both 'correct' for their methodology.
Action 1 — switch GA4 to DDA for closer comparability. After 30 days, re-measure the gap.
Action 2 — pull cross-device + view-through component of Google Ads attribution. Verify these are the gap drivers.

Action 3 — communicate to the team: '38% Google Ads + 30% GA4 = same campaigns measured by different methodologies. Choose the method that fits the decision: GA4 for cross-channel comparison; Google Ads for Smart Bidding'.
Action 4 — for budget-allocation decisions: triangulate with Lift / MMM. Neither GA4 nor Google Ads alone is incrementality.
Action 5 — long-term: build the team's measurement literacy. Different tools, different methodologies, different uses.
Avoid: declaring one 'correct' and the other 'wrong'. They're different views of the same data.
questions.explanation: Google Ads vs GA4 attribution gaps are usually methodology, not data errors. The diagnostic explains the structural difference and prescribes the use-case mapping.

questions.question: A B2B SaaS team has 4 Search campaigns segmented by funnel stage: TOFU (broad awareness), MOFU (consideration), BOFU (high-intent demo). MOFU has degraded over 90 days: CPL up 60%, MQL rate down 40%. TOFU and BOFU are fine. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: MOFU degradation while TOFU + BOFU are fine = something specific to MOFU has changed. Diagnose what's MOFU-specific.
Hypothesis 1: MOFU keywords have shifted in competitive intensity. Pull Auction Insights for MOFU specifically; check for new entrants.
Hypothesis 2: MOFU search-query mix has shifted (post-Phrase-Match-update or seasonal). Off-topic queries are flooding in, lowering MQL rate.
Hypothesis 3: MOFU landing page changed (consciously or unconsciously). Verify CRO test history; check page-load times.
Hypothesis 4: MOFU optimization event signal has shifted. If optimizing for 'Lead' but lead-quality is downstream, the algorithm has over-optimized for cheap leads at the cost of MQL rate.
Hypothesis 5: cross-campaign cannibalization. TOFU may have started capturing demand that previously triggered MOFU; users now jump from TOFU directly to BOFU, skipping MOFU.
Action 1 — pull Search Terms Report for MOFU; check for off-topic query flood.
Action 2 — pull Auction Insights for MOFU keywords; check for competitor pressure.
Action 3 — verify MOFU landing page hasn't regressed (CVR, load time, content quality).
Action 4 — pull the lead-to-MQL rate trend; if it dropped 90 days ago and stayed flat, the cause is upstream of MQL definition.
Action 5 — check optimization-event setup: if optimizing for 'Lead' on MOFU, consider switching to 'MQL' if volume permits, OR using Conversion Value Rules to weight high-quality leads.

Action 6 — diagnose cross-campaign by checking BOFU's quality: did BOFU MQL rate change? If BOFU got cleaner leads (because high-intent users skipped MOFU), MOFU's degradation may reflect demand-shift.

Action 7 — based on diagnosis: if competitive issue → invest in copy / Quality Score for MOFU; if query-mix issue → aggressive negatives; if landing-page issue → CRO; if optimization-signal issue → upgrade event.
Communicate: 'isolating the MOFU-specific cause; will have action plan in 5 business days'.
questions.explanation: Funnel-stage degradation requires stage-specific diagnosis. The diagnostic walks through stage-isolated possibilities (auction, query, LP, optimization, cross-campaign).

questions.question: A team noticed: their Smart Bidding tCPA $25 campaign had a single day last week where CPA was $58 (vs typical $24). Volume was normal that day. The team wants to know what happened. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: A single-day CPA spike with normal volume = a few high-cost auctions skewed the daily average. Diagnose the auction-specific anomaly.
Pull the day's auction data: what time did the spike occur? Was there a peak hour with unusual auction activity?
Pull the day's competitor activity: did a major competitor temporarily increase bids? Check Auction Insights for that day specifically.
Pull the day's audience composition: did the algorithm serve to a different geographic / demographic mix that day?
Pull conversion-event timing: did the spike correspond to a marketing email or external traffic surge that triggered Smart Bidding to bid up?
Hypothesis 1: a competitor ran a sale / promotion that day, raising auction prices industry-wide. CPA spike is market-driven, not campaign-driven.
Hypothesis 2: the algorithm's prediction model over-reacted to a temporary signal (e.g., an unusual recent conversion pattern). Smart Bidding occasionally has 'experimental' bid days as it explores.
Hypothesis 3: an external event (news, weather, holiday) created unusual user behavior, skewing CVR temporarily.
Action 1 — pull Auction Insights for the day; check for unusual competitor pressure.
Action 2 — pull industry CPC trend; check for market-wide spikes.
Action 3 — pull GA4 for the day; check for unusual user-behavior patterns.
Action 4 — verify the day's data quality (no tracking outage, no tag fires, no Smart Bidding 'limited' state).
Action 5 — recognize: single-day anomalies are normal in Smart Bidding. The algorithm makes per-auction bids; some days will produce outliers. Don't react to single-day spikes.
Action 6 — only act if the pattern persists for >3 days. Single-day spikes self-correct.
Action 7 — set monitoring threshold: alert only if 3-day-rolling CPA exceeds 1.5x target. Daily noise alerts cause over-reaction.
Communicate: 'single-day spike normal; investigated for cause; algorithm corrected naturally; no action needed'.
questions.explanation: Single-day Smart Bidding spikes are normal noise. Reactive adjustments to single-day data create more harm than good. The diagnostic teaches the right monitoring threshold.

questions.question: A retailer running both PMax and Standard Shopping in parallel sees their Standard Shopping campaign suddenly drop spend by 60% over 14 days, with no edits to either campaign. PMax spend is unchanged. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: Standard Shopping spend dropping while PMax is stable usually means PMax is preempting Standard Shopping's auctions. Verify and confirm.

Google's documented behavior: PMax has priority over Standard Shopping when both serve the same products. PMax decides whether to enter an auction; if it does, Standard Shopping is excluded from that auction.

Hypothesis 1: PMax's algorithm shifted to bid more aggressively over the past 14 days (e.g., due to seasonality, recent improved performance, or expanded asset groups). The shift preempts more Standard Shopping auctions.
Hypothesis 2: PMax expanded its product coverage (e.g., more SKUs in asset groups), preempting more Standard Shopping inventory.
Hypothesis 3: feed approval changes — Standard Shopping may have lost product approvals. Check Merchant Center for disapprovals.
Verify: pull the spend trend by campaign over 30 days. Confirm PMax's spend trajectory; identify when Standard Shopping started dropping.
Verify: check Merchant Center disapprovals — did 60% of Standard Shopping's products get disapproved? Unlikely but possible.
Verify: pull search-impression-share for Standard Shopping. If IS dropped to <20%, PMax is winning the auctions.
Action 1 — verify the diagnosis: pull per-campaign auction data + IS trends.

Action 2 — strategic decision: if PMax is winning more auctions and total revenue is flat or up, the shift is positive (PMax may be more efficient at Shopping). If revenue is down, PMax may be over-claiming attribution while losing actual sales.

Action 3 — measure incrementality: run a 14-day pause-test of PMax in 30% of geos. Measure total revenue + Standard Shopping recovery. If Standard Shopping recovers full spend in pause-geos AND total revenue is unchanged, PMax was just shuffling. If revenue drops in pause-geos, PMax was adding value.
Action 4 — based on test: if PMax is incremental, accept the Standard Shopping reduction. If PMax is shuffling, restructure (e.g., make PMax feed-only or remove the overlap).
Action 5 — long-term monitoring: track the PMax / Standard Shopping spend balance monthly. Drift in balance signals algorithm preference shifts.
Communicate: 'PMax preemption is documented behavior; running incrementality test to verify it's adding value vs just shuffling spend'.
questions.explanation: PMax / Standard Shopping interaction is governed by PMax's priority. Spend shifts may be value-add or value-shuffle; only incrementality testing reveals which.

questions.question: A team's Brand Search campaign suddenly shows 3x the impression count over 7 days but stable click count and conversion count. CTR dropped from 18% to 6%. Walk through diagnosis.
answer_criteria: Brand campaign with 3x impressions + stable clicks/conversions + CTR drop = something is causing irrelevant impressions. Diagnose what's triggering the campaign on non-brand queries.
Hypothesis 1: Phrase / Broad match keywords on brand keywords are catching close-variant queries that aren't actually brand searches.
Hypothesis 2: A new ad group with broader brand-related keywords was added (verify change log).
Hypothesis 3: Match-type updates from Google have started catching new query types.

Verify: pull Search Terms Report for Brand campaign. Look at the recent week's queries vs prior weeks. Are there new query patterns ('similar to [brand]', 'alternative to [brand]') that shouldn't trigger Brand?
Verify: check Auction Insights — did a competitor run a 'compare to [brand]' campaign that triggers your brand on their queries?
Verify: pull match-type distribution for Brand keywords. Are any in Phrase or Broad match (more permissive) when they should be Exact?
Action 1 — pull Search Terms Report; categorize the new queries.
Action 2 — add Negative Keywords for irrelevant patterns (e.g., 'jobs at [brand]', 'how to spell [brand]', 'alternatives to [brand]').
Action 3 — tighten match types where possible. Brand keywords often work best in Exact match; the close-variant matching still catches misspellings.
Action 4 — verify the underlying brand search-volume hasn't changed (Google Trends). If brand volume is stable, the impression spike is purely match-broadening, not actual demand.
Action 5 — measure post-fix: CTR should recover to 15%+ within 7 days as irrelevant queries are filtered out.
Action 6 — long-term: monthly Search Terms hygiene for Brand campaigns; brand drift accumulates without maintenance.
Communicate: 'brand match-broadening; cleaning up via negatives + match-type tightening; expect CTR recovery in 7-10 days'.
questions.explanation: Brand campaign CTR drops with stable clicks indicate match-type broadening capturing non-brand queries. The diagnostic prescribes negative-keyword cleanup + match-type tightening.

questions.question: A lead-gen campaign optimizing for 'Lead' event has stable performance for 3 months at $40 CPL. Last month: CPL improved to $28 (good news!) but the sales team complains lead quality dropped — fewer of those leads convert to MQLs. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: CPL improvement + MQL rate decline = the algorithm shifted to cheaper but lower-quality leads. Diagnose what changed in the optimization signal.
Hypothesis 1: Smart Bidding's algorithm found a cheaper-acquisition audience segment but those users have lower buying intent. Common when optimizing for top-of-funnel events.
Hypothesis 2: a recent ad copy or landing page change increased lead volume but at lower-intent threshold (e.g., shorter form attracts more lower-intent submissions).
Hypothesis 3: Google's algorithm received a recent update / re-training that shifted prediction patterns.
Hypothesis 4: a new audience segment was added or audience signals changed.
Verify: pull lead-source segmentation. Did a new audience or campaign type get added? Did one of the existing audiences shift composition?
Verify: pull the lead form / landing page change history. Did anyone simplify the form recently?
Verify: pull Smart Bidding's optimization-event configuration. Is the Lead event still the right level? Should it be upgraded to MQL or weighted with Conversion Value Rules?
Action 1 — diagnose root cause via the change log + lead-source breakdown.
Action 2 — if lead form was simplified: revert OR add qualification fields back to maintain lead quality.

Action 3 — if Smart Bidding signal is the issue: implement Conversion Value Rules to weight high-quality leads (e.g., enterprise leads at 3x). The algorithm will then prefer high-quality leads at higher CPL.
Action 4 — long-term: upgrade optimization to MQL once volume permits (≥30/wk). Optimizing on MQL ensures quality alignment.
Action 5 — set up a feedback loop: monthly review of CPL vs MQL-rate vs SQL-rate trend. Single-metric (CPL) optimization without quality feedback is the failure mode.
Action 6 — communicate to sales: 'fixing optimization signal to prefer quality over quantity; expect CPL to rise back to $40 but MQL rate to recover'.
Avoid: celebrating CPL improvement without checking quality.
questions.explanation: CPL improvement + MQL decline is a classic 'cheaper but worse leads' pattern caused by misaligned optimization signal. The diagnostic prescribes signal correction (Value Rules, optimization upgrade).

questions.question: A retailer's PMax campaign performance dropped 30% over 7 days. Spend, audience, creative, bidding all unchanged. The team can't find what changed. Walk through where to look.
answer_criteria: 30% performance drop in 7 days with no campaign-side changes = upstream issue. Diagnose systematically.
Check 1 — Conversion tracking: did a recent web update break pixel firing on key pages? Verify Purchase event volume in Events Manager + GA4.
Check 2 — Merchant Center: any recent disapprovals? Pull product-disapproval trend over the past 14 days.
Check 3 — Feed quality: were product titles, prices, or images updated recently? Even improvements can disrupt the algorithm temporarily.
Check 4 — Auction Insights: did a major competitor enter or escalate? Pull the past 14 days of competitive data.
Check 5 — Macro factors: is industry-wide CPM up? Is consumer demand down? Pull Google Trends for the brand's category.
Check 6 — Product mix: did a top-selling product go out of stock or get removed from the feed? PMax may be losing its top-revenue contributor.
Check 7 — Asset group changes: were any asset group assets removed or marked low-performing?
Action 1 — start with conversion tracking (highest impact, fastest verification).
Action 2 — check Merchant Center for disapprovals — pull the diff in product-approval state over 14 days.
Action 3 — check inventory: pull the top-50 SKUs from the past month and verify they're still in the feed and in stock.
Action 4 — check Auction Insights for competitor pressure shifts.
Action 5 — check the broader market: GA4 organic conversion rate, Google Trends for category demand.
Action 6 — based on diagnosis: fix the specific issue (re-enable disapproved products, restock top SKUs, etc.).
Action 7 — DON'T make Smart Bidding changes; the issue is upstream of bidding.
Action 8 — set up monitoring: daily alerts for >5% spend or conversion changes; daily Merchant Center disapproval rate; daily inventory of top-50 SKUs.
Communicate: 'isolating root cause via systematic check; will identify within 24h'.
questions.explanation: PMax performance drops with no campaign-side changes require upstream diagnosis (tracking, feed, inventory, market, competitor). The diagnostic walks the systematic priority order.

questions.question: A team's Google Ads account has 12 campaigns, 6 of which are below 30 conversions/month. Smart Bidding is in 'Limited' state on those 6. The team thinks they should consolidate — but isn't sure into what. Walk through the diagnostic and structuring decision.
answer_criteria: Below 30 conv/month puts campaigns into Limited Smart Bidding state — algorithm is signal-starved. Consolidation can help, but only when economics align.
Step 1 — Audit the 6 low-volume campaigns. What are the unit economics, target audiences, and objectives? Group into clusters where economics + audience + objective align.

Step 2 — Identify consolidation candidates: campaigns within the same cluster (similar economics, similar audience, similar objective) are good candidates. Campaigns across clusters (different objectives) should remain separate.
Step 3 — Hypothesize the right structure: probably 2-3 consolidated campaigns from the original 6, plus 6 unchanged campaigns above the volume threshold. Total: ~8-9 campaigns.
Step 4 — Check the high-volume 6 for redundancy too. Are some of them duplicating coverage that consolidation would help?
Step 5 — Implement the consolidation in stages: combine 2 campaigns first, measure for 4 weeks, then iterate. Reduces risk of consolidation-shock.

Step 6 — Watch for: consolidation may force compromise targets (different unit economics getting one tROAS). If consolidating campaigns with truly different economics, plan to use Conversion Value Rules to differentiate within the consolidated campaign.
Step 7 — Measure: post-consolidation conversion volume should clear the 30/month threshold; CPA should be similar to pre-consolidation; Smart Bidding should exit Limited.
Action 1 — pull per-campaign data on volume, economics, objective.
Action 2 — design the target structure (2-3 from the 6, plus the existing high-volume 6).
Action 3 — implement first consolidation; measure 4 weeks.
Action 4 — iterate based on data.
Action 5 — document the rationale for each remaining campaign (why it's separate).
Avoid: consolidating campaigns with truly different economics into one — forces averaging that under-serves both.
Communicate: 'staged consolidation, measuring at each step, target structure has clear rationale per campaign'.
questions.explanation: Account consolidation requires economic-cluster mapping, staged implementation, and measurement at each step. Bulk consolidation risks compromising distinct economics.

questions.question: A team running 200 negative keywords across an account sees a Search Terms Report showing 'free [product]' queries appearing despite 'free' being a long-time negative. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: 'Free' is a negative but related queries are still appearing = the negative isn't matching the actual query patterns. Diagnose the match-type issue.
Negative keyword match types: broad-negative blocks queries CONTAINING the term; phrase-negative blocks specific phrase; exact-negative blocks ONLY the exact term.
Hypothesis 1: 'free' was added as exact-negative, which only blocks queries that are literally 'free' alone. Queries like 'free [product]' or '[product] free' still trigger.
Hypothesis 2: 'free' was added as phrase-negative ('free' in a specific position) and the queries appearing have it in different positions.
Hypothesis 3: the negative was applied at the ad-group level but the query is happening at the campaign or other ad-group level.
Verify: pull the negative keyword list. Confirm 'free' is broad-negative (most permissive blocking). If it's exact or phrase, expand to broad.
Verify: confirm the negative is applied at the right level (account-level Negative Keyword List or campaign-level).
Action 1 — change 'free' to broad-negative: this blocks any query containing 'free'.
Action 2 — apply via Negative Keyword List in Shared Library so it covers all relevant campaigns.
Action 3 — measure the impact: 'free' queries should disappear from Search Terms within 24-48 hours.
Action 4 — audit other negatives for similar match-type issues. Common pattern: 'jobs', 'careers', 'how to', 'tutorial', 'review' should all be broad-negatives.
Action 5 — set up a weekly Search Terms hygiene review process. Identify new patterns of irrelevant queries; add as broad-negatives.
Action 6 — long-term: maintain a 'standard negatives' list (free, jobs, tutorial, DIY, etc.) that's applied to all new campaigns by default.
Communicate: 'match-type issue; fixing in 5 minutes; hygiene process update prevents recurrence'.
questions.explanation: Negative keyword match types matter. Exact-negative is the most restrictive; broad-negative is the most permissive blocker. The diagnostic identifies the match-type miscalibration.

questions.question: A PMax retailer's spend allocation: 78% Shopping placements, 12% YouTube, 8% Display, 2% Discover/Gmail. ROAS 4.5x. Last 14 days: YouTube share rose to 35%, Shopping fell to 55%. ROAS dropped to 3.2x. Team wants to know what shifted.
answer_criteria: Channel-mix shift toward YouTube + ROAS drop = PMax algorithm allocated more spend to lower-immediate-ROAS placements. Diagnose why.
Hypothesis 1: new YouTube creative assets were uploaded, giving the algorithm new options. The algorithm tested them, found promise, allocated more spend.
Hypothesis 2: Shopping inventory dropped (feed disapprovals, inventory out-of-stock), forcing the algorithm to find conversion volume elsewhere — YouTube became the alternative.
Hypothesis 3: PMax algorithm explored a new optimization path that emphasizes YouTube's audience-discovery benefit. The 14-day shift may be exploration, not stable preference.
Hypothesis 4: external factors — competitive pressure on Shopping increased CPMs, making YouTube relatively more cost-effective.
Verify: check Merchant Center disapprovals + inventory in stock for top-revenue products.
Verify: check asset group changes — were new YouTube creatives recently added?
Verify: check Auction Insights for Shopping; did competitor pressure rise?
Action 1 — verify Shopping inventory + feed health (most likely cause).
Action 2 — if inventory is fine: the algorithm is exploring; give it 7-10 days to stabilize. Don't immediately intervene.
Action 3 — if persistent shift after 14 days: assess whether YouTube spend is genuinely incremental (drives downstream brand demand) or wasted. Run a 14-day geo holdout test on PMax.
Action 4 — if YouTube is genuinely valuable: accept the shift; ROAS recovery may take 4-6 weeks as the algorithm optimizes the new mix.
Action 5 — if YouTube spend is wasted: implement asset-group-level controls to limit YouTube exposure (reduce YouTube creative depth, focus on Shopping-feed assets).
Action 6 — set up channel-mix monitoring: alert if any channel's share shifts >10% week-over-week. Investigate before accepting drift.
Communicate: 'channel-mix shift identified; investigating cause; action depends on whether shift is value-creating or value-destroying'.
questions.explanation: PMax channel-mix shifts require root-cause diagnosis (inventory, creative, competitive). Action depends on whether the shift creates or destroys value.

questions.question: A team's tCPA campaign hit target $40 reliably for 3 months. They added Conversion Value Rules to weight new customers at 1.5x last week. Now: CPA shifted to $48, but the algorithm is reporting strong NCA growth. Walk through evaluation.
answer_criteria: CPA up + NCA growth = algorithm is now optimizing for the weighted-value metric, finding more new customers at higher CPA. Diagnose if this is the right tradeoff.

Verify: pull NCA count pre vs post-rules. If NCA went from 100 to 130 monthly with CPA $40 → $48, the CAC for new customers improved (40 + 50% volume vs 48 + 30% volume = some math depending on prior NCA mix).

Compute new-customer CAC: monthly NCA spend / monthly NCA count. If pre-rules new-customer CAC was $80 (because 50% of conversions were new) and post-rules is $74 (because more conversions are weighted-new), the rules are doing what was intended.
Verify: pull non-NCA (returning customer) volume change. If returning volume dropped, the algorithm is correctly de-prioritizing repeat conversions in favor of new.

Stated business value: if NCA is the strategic priority + LTV is materially higher for new vs repeat, the rules are correctly applied; the headline CPA increase reflects strategic alignment, not degradation.
Action 1 — recompute success metrics around new-customer CAC, not blended CPA. Educate the team on the new framework.
Action 2 — verify the LTV differential: are new customers actually 1.5x more valuable downstream? If yes, the weight is justified. If no, recalibrate the weight.
Action 3 — measure 90-day repeat rate of new customers: are post-rule NCAs as valuable as pre-rule NCAs? Weight should be adjusted if new-customer LTV is shifting.
Action 4 — set the team's expectation: with Conversion Value Rules, blended CPA is no longer the right success metric. Track weighted-value-per-spend and NCA-cost.
Action 5 — long-term: quarterly recalibration of weights as customer-cohort LTV data matures.
Communicate to leadership: 'rule weights are working as designed; we're acquiring more high-value customers at slightly higher per-conversion CPA; this aligns with strategic priority'.
questions.explanation: Conversion Value Rules shift the optimization target. CPA appears to degrade but NCA-cost should improve. The diagnostic reframes success around the right metric.

questions.question: A team running campaigns with 30-day click attribution sees their Smart Bidding tCPA performance reported correctly for 60+ days. Then they shorten attribution to 7-day click. 14 days later: tCPA reports degraded performance (CPA up 20%) but Shopify shows revenue stable. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: Shortening attribution window from 30 to 7 days = Smart Bidding loses ~70% of its conversion data feedback (most conversions happen within 7 days, but the long tail past 7 days is now invisible to the algorithm).
Result: Smart Bidding optimizes against fewer conversions per click → predicted CPA is overstated → algorithm bids more conservatively → reported CPA appears higher.
The Shopify-stable signal confirms: actual user behavior unchanged; revenue unchanged. The 'CPA increase' is purely an attribution-method change.
Verify: pull Smart Bidding's reported conversion-window data. If conversion volume dropped 20-30% in reported terms while Shopify is stable, the gap is the now-invisible 8-30 day conversion tail.
Verify: pull conversion-time-distribution data. What % of conversions happen within 7 days vs 8-30 days? For most B2C brands, 70-85% within 7 days; B2B can be 30-50% within 7 days.
Action 1 — explain to team: 'attribution window change creates apparent CPA increase but actual performance is unchanged. The algorithm now only sees 70-85% of true conversions and bids accordingly.'

Action 2 — recalibrate Smart Bidding tCPA targets: if previous target was $40 (against 30-day attribution), new target should be ~$32 to maintain effective 30-day-equivalent CPA (since the algorithm now bids based on smaller visible conversion pool).
Action 3 — DON'T tighten the target further (that compounds the volume loss); recalibrate to match the new attribution scope.
Action 4 — alternative: revert to 30-day attribution if 7-day was an experiment, not a strategic change.
Action 5 — set the team's expectation: changing attribution windows is a high-impact decision; pre-validate that the new window matches the true conversion-cycle distribution.
Action 6 — long-term: align attribution window with median click-to-conversion delay. For most B2C, 30 days is appropriate; for B2B, longer; for impulse purchases, shorter.
Communicate: 'attribution change explains the CPA shift; recalibrating target to maintain effective economics'.
questions.explanation: Attribution window changes affect Smart Bidding's visibility into conversions. Shorter windows underweight long-cycle conversions, requiring target recalibration.

questions.question: A retailer's Shopping campaign sees 40% of impressions on a product the brand wants to deprioritize (low margin, focus on premium products). Diagnose how to redirect spend without disabling the product.
answer_criteria: The algorithm is allocating to the low-margin product based on its conversion data (likely high CVR, attractive price). To redirect, change the algorithm's signal, not the product.
Hypothesis: the low-margin product has historical strong conversion data, so the algorithm preferentially serves it. Without intervention, this continues.
Strategy 1 — Conversion Value Rules: weight high-margin products higher (e.g., 1.5x) so the algorithm's optimization favors them despite similar conversion-volume signals.

Strategy 2 — campaign segmentation: separate Shopping into multiple campaigns by margin tier. Low-margin products in their own campaign with lower priority + tighter tROAS; high-margin in a separate campaign with higher priority + broader tROAS.

Strategy 3 — feed-level signal: ensure the product feed accurately represents margin (via custom labels). Use custom labels in Listing Group bidding (in Standard Shopping) or asset group filters (in PMax).
Strategy 4 — modify the product's bid via custom label hierarchy in Standard Shopping; can also down-prioritize in PMax via campaign segmentation.
Verify: pull the product's CVR, AOV, and margin data. Confirm the algorithm's preference is data-driven (not bias).
Action 1 — implement Conversion Value Rules to weight high-margin products higher (lowest-effort, fastest impact).
Action 2 — if needed, restructure Shopping into margin-tiered campaigns.
Action 3 — verify the feed's custom labels correctly identify high-margin products.
Action 4 — measure: 30-day impact on product-mix spend allocation. Target shift toward high-margin products without total revenue loss.
Action 5 — DON'T disable the low-margin product entirely if it has strategic value (loss-leader, attach-rate driver). Just rebalance.
Action 6 — long-term: monthly product-mix review; rebalance as new products launch.
Communicate: 'algorithmic preference reflects performance data; rebalancing via Value Rules + segmentation; expect 30-day impact'.
questions.explanation: Product-mix optimization in Shopping requires algorithm-signal manipulation (Value Rules, campaign segmentation), not product disabling. The diagnostic prescribes the leverage points.

questions.question: A B2B SaaS company's Search campaign on tCPA $200 has been delivering at $185 reliably. The CMO requests target be lowered to $150 to 'force efficiency improvements'. Diagnose the likely outcome.
answer_criteria: Tightening tCPA from $200 to $150 = 25% tighter target. Smart Bidding's typical response: 'Limited Learning' state for 1-2 weeks, conversion volume drops 30-40%, CPA settles around $160-$175 (close to but not at new target).
Mechanism: at $200 target, the algorithm filters auctions to those predicted to clear $200. At $150 target, much smaller pool of auctions clear the new threshold; volume drops accordingly.
Verify: pull current auction inventory data. What % of current $185-CPA conversions are below $150 marginal? If only 30%, the new target is impractical.
Verify: pull conversion-volume sensitivity. Has the team's tested previous tCPA-tightening experiments? Historical data on how the algorithm responded.
Hypothesis: setting target $50 below current natural performance always loses volume + rarely hits the new target. The CMO's expectation is misaligned with Smart Bidding mechanics.

Action 1 — counter-recommend: don't go from $200 to $150. Instead, propose: $185 → $175 in 2 weeks (small tightening, observe); then $175 → $165 if the first step works; iterate to find the sustainable target.
Action 2 — explain to CMO: 'target $150 is achievable IF underlying inputs improve (creative quality, landing-page CVR, audience targeting). Without those changes, $150 will produce 60% volume loss.'

Action 3 — invest in the underlying levers: which interventions could lower CPA without losing volume? CRO on landing page (typically 10-30% CVR lift = 10-30% CPA reduction), creative refresh (10-20% improvement), tighter audience targeting.
Action 4 — set realistic timeline: lowering tCPA by 25% sustainably typically requires 60-90 days of underlying improvement work, not a target change overnight.

Action 5 — measure intermediate results: at each iteration step, document conversion volume + CPA. If the campaign converges at $165 stably, that's the new floor. Going below requires more underlying work.
Action 6 — communicate to CMO: 'we'll work toward $150 over 90 days via underlying improvements + staged target tightening. Single-step from $200 to $150 will fail.'
questions.explanation: Aggressive tCPA tightening always loses volume + rarely hits the new target. The diagnostic prescribes staged tightening + underlying-input improvement, not target-only change.

questions.question: A retailer running PMax sees the campaign hitting tROAS but spending only 60% of daily budget for 14 days running. The algorithm seems content but the budget is unused. Diagnose.
answer_criteria: Underspending while hitting tROAS = the algorithm can't find enough auctions clearing the tROAS target to consume the budget.
Hypothesis 1: the tROAS target is too aggressive given current auction conditions. Loosening would unlock more auctions.
Hypothesis 2: the audience pool / inventory is constrained. Even at any tROAS, there isn't enough auction volume in the targeted audience to spend the full budget.
Hypothesis 3: feed inventory is limited (some products out of stock or disapproved), reducing the auction surface.
Hypothesis 4: competitive pressure — auction prices have risen above the tROAS-eligibility threshold for many auctions.
Verify: check Auction Insights — has competitor pressure increased? Are CPMs up?
Verify: check Merchant Center — feed health, disapprovals, inventory.
Verify: check current tROAS achievement rate — if hitting 5x consistently, can the algorithm find more 5x-eligible auctions if loosened to 4.5x?
Action 1 — verify feed health and inventory first.
Action 2 — if feed is fine: the issue is target calibration. Loosen tROAS by 10-15% (e.g., 5x → 4.3x) and observe over 7 days.
Action 3 — measure: at the looser target, does spend increase? Does ROAS settle at the new lower target? If yes, the constraint was tROAS, not market.

Action 4 — strategic decision: choose between (a) accept underspend at 5x (capital not deployed but ROAS protected), (b) loosen target for more spend at lower ROAS (capital deployed but lower ROI), (c) invest in expanding the auction surface (more asset groups, audience signals, geo expansion).
Action 5 — quantify: if loosening to 4.3x produces 80% budget utilization at $X total revenue vs current 60% at $Y total revenue, calculate net contribution to decide.
Action 6 — DO NOT increase the budget further while underspending; that doesn't solve the binding constraint.
Communicate: 'identified the binding constraint (tROAS too tight); deciding between protect-ROAS-or-deploy-capital tradeoff'.
questions.explanation: PMax underspending at hit tROAS = target is too tight for current auction conditions. The diagnostic identifies the binding constraint and prescribes the tradeoff decision.

questions.question: A team's non-brand campaign with 100 keywords sees Search Terms Report showing 60% of queries are 'close variants' (synonyms, plurals, same-intent variations). The team wants to tighten match types to Exact-only to regain control. Diagnose the trade-off.
answer_criteria: Exact-match still triggers close variants (synonyms, plurals, same-intent queries). Tightening to Exact-only doesn't fully regain control over what queries trigger ads — it's a trade-off, not a clean fix.
Pre-2018 Exact match was strict; modern Exact triggers ~20-40% close variants by Google's design. Total query control is impossible without negative-keyword discipline.
Hypothesis 1: the team wants control for legitimate reasons (off-topic queries are wasting spend). Solution is aggressive negative keywords + tighter ad-group themes, not match-type-only change.
Hypothesis 2: the team wants control for paranoid reasons. Negative impact: tightening reduces volume by 30-50% (Smart Bidding loses signal density from broader query pool).

Verify: pull the 'close variant' queries — are they actually irrelevant or are they valid same-intent queries the team would want? Many close variants are valuable (e.g., 'mens running shoes' triggering on 'running shoes for men').
Verify: pull the conversion data on close-variant queries. Are they converting? If yes, they're valuable — tightening would lose those conversions.
Action 1 — pull a 60-day breakdown of close-variant queries: which are converting, which are wasting spend.
Action 2 — for converting close variants: keep them flowing (don't tighten). They're valuable.
Action 3 — for wasting close variants: add as negatives. This regains control without losing valuable variants.
Action 4 — if some core keywords have many wasting variants: switch THOSE to Exact (not all). Selective tightening.
Action 5 — Smart Bidding's broad+match-type combination: works best with mixed match types. Don't go all-Exact unless very specific brand-protection needs require it.
Action 6 — long-term: weekly Search Terms hygiene + selective match-type tightening for keyword-specific issues.
Communicate: 'close-variant control isn't binary; selective tightening + aggressive negatives is the right approach'.
questions.explanation: Match-type-only tightening trades control for volume. Selective tightening + negative-keyword hygiene is the modern approach.

questions.question: A team running Maximize Conversion Value (no tROAS) sees the campaign's average ROAS at 6x but with high variance: individual conversions range $20-$2,000 in value. The CFO wants to set a tROAS target to 'reduce variance'. Diagnose what would happen.
answer_criteria: Setting a tROAS target on a high-variance campaign = the algorithm filters out the low-value-end of the distribution. Variance reduces; total revenue may also decrease.
Mechanism: the campaign captures the full value distribution because there's no constraint. With tROAS, the algorithm only pursues conversions predicted to clear the target.

If tROAS is set at average current ROAS (6x): low-value conversions filtered out; the algorithm targets higher-value conversions only. Total volume drops; AOV per conversion rises; total revenue may drop.
If tROAS is set conservatively (e.g., 3x): captures most of the current distribution + filters only the lowest-value tail. Variance reduces modestly; revenue mostly preserved.
Verify: pull the conversion-value distribution. What % of revenue is in the bottom quartile vs top? If top quartile drives 70% of value, filtering bottom is low-cost.
Hypothesis: the CFO wants 'predictability' (lower variance) but the cost is total revenue. This is a tradeoff, not a free improvement.

Action 1 — model the impact: at tROAS 6x, expect 20-30% volume drop, 5-15% revenue drop, but more consistent CPA + ROAS week-over-week. At tROAS 3x, expect 5-10% volume drop, minimal revenue drop, modestly improved variance.
Action 2 — present the trade-off to CFO: 'lower variance costs revenue; the right target depends on whether predictability or growth is priority.'
Action 3 — recommended: tROAS 3x (modest filtering, preserves volume) for first 30 days; then evaluate. Don't jump to tROAS 6x immediately.

Action 4 — alternative: keep Maximize Conversion Value (high variance is the cost of high revenue) + use Conversion Value Rules to weight predictable / desirable conversions higher. This shapes optimization without filtering hard.
Action 5 — long-term: revisit quarterly. The right tROAS target evolves as auction conditions and audience saturation shift.
Communicate: 'reducing variance has a cost in volume; designing the lowest-cost approach for the desired predictability level'.
questions.explanation: Setting tROAS reduces variance but trades volume. The diagnostic models the trade-off and recommends the lowest-cost approach to the desired predictability.

questions.question: A retailer launched a new PMax campaign 6 weeks ago. Reported ROAS: 5x. Internal MMM analysis: PMax is delivering 25% incremental ROAS. The CFO is excited about the 5x; the marketing team is concerned about the 1.25x incremental. Walk through the framing.
answer_criteria: 5x reported vs 1.25x incremental = 75% non-incremental conversions. PMax is taking credit for many conversions that would have happened anyway via brand search, organic, or other channels.
Common pattern: PMax's reported ROAS is inflated by brand-cannibalization, view-through-attribution, and overlap with existing demand sources. 1.25x incremental is genuinely concerning.
Verify: are PMax brand exclusions enabled? If not, much of the gap is brand-search cannibalization. Implement and re-measure.
Verify: pull the PMax conversion-source breakdown. Are conversions concentrated on brand-search-style queries (per Insights tab)? On retargeting audiences?
Verify: validate the MMM methodology. Is the 25% incremental rate based on geo holdouts or time-series modeling? Geo holdouts are more reliable.
Hypothesis 1: PMax is mostly capturing demand that would have happened — true incrementality is low. Action: scale down, redirect budget to channels with higher incrementality.
Hypothesis 2: MMM is underestimating PMax's true contribution (e.g., long-tail effects not captured). Action: re-validate MMM methodology before scaling down.
Hypothesis 3: PMax is incremental but only at smaller scale; current $X spend is in the saturated zone. Action: test a 30-50% scale-down; measure incrementality at lower spend.
Action 1 — verify brand exclusions are correctly applied. If not, fix and re-measure incrementality after 30 days.
Action 2 — run a geo holdout of PMax for 21 days; measure true incremental contribution. This is the load-bearing test.

Action 3 — based on holdout: if incrementality is confirmed low, scale down PMax 30-50% and redirect to higher-incrementality channels (Demand Gen, Brand Search at appropriate impression-share floor).
Action 4 — if PMax is genuinely incremental at lower scale: keep it at the optimized scale; don't over-invest beyond the productive zone.
Action 5 — communicate to CFO: 'PMax reports 5x but true incremental contribution is lower. Adjusting spend to maximize true contribution rather than reported ROAS.'
Action 6 — long-term: quarterly incrementality re-measurement; PMax incrementality shifts as competitive + organic landscapes evolve.
questions.explanation: PMax's reported ROAS often vastly exceeds incremental ROAS. The diagnostic forces incrementality measurement + adjustment to maximize true contribution.

questions.question: A SaaS team's Google Search account has 5 campaigns aggregating to $30K/month and 200 leads. They built a 6th campaign for a new product line ($5K/month). After 30 days, the new campaign delivers 0 conversions despite reasonable traffic. Walk through diagnosis.
answer_criteria: New campaign at $5K + reasonable traffic + 0 conversions = optimization signal failure. Diagnose where the chain is broken.
Check 1 — Conversion tracking: is the new product's conversion event configured + firing? Test a manual conversion to verify.
Check 2 — Optimization-event setup: is the campaign optimizing for the right event? If the new product has different conversion-funnel stages, the optimization may be misconfigured.
Check 3 — Landing page: is the LP for the new product converting at all? Pull GA4 LP-CVR data. If LP-CVR is 0, conversion isn't possible regardless of bidding.
Check 4 — Audience fit: is the targeted audience right for the new product? If it's a B2B product targeted at consumer audience, no conversions despite traffic.
Check 5 — Smart Bidding learning: campaign is in Learning Phase (only 30 days at $5K = ~$150/day); below typical threshold for Smart Bidding to converge.
Verify: check the conversion-tracking setup for the new campaign first.
Verify: check LP-CVR via GA4 + heatmap analysis.
Verify: pull traffic-source segmentation; ensure traffic is from intended audience.
Action 1 — verify conversion tracking (highest priority).
Action 2 — verify LP CVR via independent metric (GA4).
Action 3 — verify audience targeting matches the product's target market.

Action 4 — if all infrastructure is correct: increase budget temporarily ($150/day → $400/day) to give Smart Bidding more conversion data faster. 30 conversions/month is the rough threshold; need to hit that to exit Limited Learning.

Action 5 — alternative: switch optimization event to upper-funnel (e.g., 'Initiate Trial' or 'Add to Cart') temporarily to gather signal faster, then upgrade to the strategic event after 4 weeks of data.
Action 6 — set 14-day measurement window: after fixes, expect first conversions within 14 days. If still 0, deeper diagnostic needed.
Action 7 — long-term: new campaigns require infrastructure validation BEFORE launch (tracking, LP CVR, audience fit). Pre-launch checklist would catch most issues.
Communicate: 'systematically diagnosing infrastructure; fixing root cause before scaling spend; will update in 5 business days'.
questions.explanation: New-campaign 0-conversion failures usually trace to tracking, LP CVR, audience fit, or insufficient budget for Learning. The diagnostic walks the priority order.

questions.question: You manage Google Ads for a B2C ecommerce brand: $400K monthly across Brand ($30K, 18x ROAS), Non-Brand ($150K, 3.5x ROAS), PMax ($150K, 4.5x ROAS), Demand Gen ($70K, 2.8x ROAS). Total blended 4.2x. CFO offers $200K incremental budget. Build the allocation framework.
answer_criteria: Naïve approach: 'add to PMax (highest ROAS after Brand)'. Reality: each channel has different incrementality, marginal ROAS curves, and unit economics.

Brand Search 18x is largely non-incremental — most users would convert organically. Adding $X to Brand mostly buys conversions you'd get anyway. Brand has fixed query volume; even doubling spend would yield diminishing returns within weeks.
Non-Brand 3.5x is genuinely incremental but at saturation: $150K spend already captures most volume; +50% spend likely yields 25-35% volume lift due to marginal-CPC rise.

PMax 4.5x reported but likely 60-70% incremental (some brand cannibalization, view-through inflation): true incremental ROAS ~3.0x. Marginal economics on +50% spend: similar volume curve to Non-Brand.
Demand Gen 2.8x reported but high-incrementality (almost entirely upper-funnel new demand). Marginal incremental ROAS may be 2.2x but with strong feeding-funnel benefit.

Recommended allocation: $100K to Non-Brand (mature but incremental, ~$300K incremental revenue), $60K to PMax (improve incrementality via optimization, ~$180K incremental), $30K to Demand Gen (upper-funnel feeding, drives downstream Brand demand), $10K to Brand only if maintaining impression share is at risk.
Stated assumptions: marginal ROAS at 70-85% of average; PMax with brand exclusions enabled; Demand Gen has audience expansion capacity.
Validation plan: implement allocation in 50% steps over 4 weeks. Measure marginal performance at each step. Adjust if marginal returns are below projections.
Risk callout: PMax may not reach 80% of average ROAS at +50% spend; if marginal ROAS is below 3.5x, scale back the PMax allocation.
Communicate to CFO: 'allocation respects incrementality framework, not just reported ROAS; expected total incremental revenue ~$520K vs naïve $300K from all-PMax allocation'.
questions.explanation: Cross-channel allocation requires incrementality framework, marginal-decay modeling, and Brand-specific ceiling recognition. Naïve ROAS allocation over-funds Brand and under-funds incrementality.

questions.question: A retailer's PMax has $80K spend, 5x ROAS, 78% Shopping placement spend, 60% first-time buyers. Demand Gen test (separate $20K campaign) delivers 4x ROAS, 85% first-time buyers. CFO offers $40K. Quantify allocation between PMax and Demand Gen.
answer_criteria: Compute new-customer count per spend: PMax ~480 NCAs at $80K spend = $167 per NCA. Demand Gen ~340 NCAs at $20K spend = $59 per NCA. Demand Gen is much more efficient at NCA acquisition.
Compute marginal NCA cost: PMax marginal +$20K likely 80% efficient = $200/NCA. Demand Gen marginal +$20K = $70/NCA (less marginal decay since it's earlier in growth curve).
Demand Gen has strong upper-funnel halo: it generates brand-search lift that downstream amplifies PMax + Brand Search. PMax doesn't reciprocate this benefit.
Recommended: $25K to Demand Gen (lower marginal NCA cost + halo benefit + scaling room), $15K to PMax (mature but additive at modest scale).
Project: Demand Gen +$25K = ~330 NCAs. PMax +$15K = ~75 NCAs. Total: ~405 net-new NCAs from $40K incremental spend.
Compare to all-PMax allocation: PMax +$40K = ~150 NCAs. Recommended allocation produces 2.7x more NCAs.
Stated assumptions: marginal NCA efficiency holds; Demand Gen audience expansion capacity available; brand-search halo benefit measurable but not in scope to quantify here.
Validation plan: 30-day measurement post-allocation. Demand Gen monthly NCA growth should be tracked vs prior-period baseline. PMax monthly should show modest NCA growth with no degradation.
Risk callout: if Demand Gen's marginal performance is worse than projected (could happen as it scales), shift remaining capacity back to PMax. Re-evaluate weekly during the ramp.
Communicate to CFO: 'recommendation favors NCA cost-effectiveness; Demand Gen has more growth capacity at this stage; expect 2.7x more new customers vs naïve PMax-only allocation'.
questions.explanation: Multi-channel NCA allocation requires marginal NCA cost computation + halo recognition. Demand Gen often has better NCA economics than scaling-saturated PMax.

questions.question: A team's tCPA $50 campaign delivered 1,000 conversions at $48 average CPA last month. They want to test loosening to tCPA $60 to capture more volume. Build the model for expected outcome.
answer_criteria: Loosening tCPA from $50 to $60 = 20% looser. Smart Bidding can pursue auctions where predicted CPA is $50-$60.
Expected auction-inventory unlock: typically 25-40% more inventory at +20% tCPA tolerance.
Expected new-conversion CPA: marginal conversions are by definition more expensive than current $48 avg. Likely marginal CPA: $54-$60.
Expected total volume: current 1,000 + 25-35% = 1,250-1,350 conversions at blended $51-$54 CPA.
Math: $1,250 × $52 = $65,000 spend; current 1,000 × $48 = $48,000 spend. Marginal $17K spend → 250 marginal conversions = $68 marginal CPA.

Contribution math: at $90 AOV × 35% margin = $31.50 contribution per conversion. Marginal contribution from 250 added conversions = 250 × $31.50 = $7,875. Less marginal media-cost premium (250 × ($68 - $48) = $5,000). Net marginal contribution = $7,875 - $5,000 = $2,875/month additional.
Decision: positive marginal contribution makes the test worthwhile. $2,875/month at modest risk.
Risk: actual marginal CPA could be higher than $68, in which case net contribution shrinks or becomes negative. Bound the risk by setting a stop-loss: revert if marginal CPA exceeds $75.
Test design: implement tCPA $60 for 21 days; measure actual marginal CPA + volume; decide based on observed economics.
Stated assumptions: marginal-auction-inventory elasticity at +20% target; AOV stable; margin stable; no creative or audience changes confound the period.
Recommendation: run the test. Expected positive contribution ~$2,875/month at bounded risk; downside is reversible.
Communicate: 'test is data-driven; modest expected upside with reversible downside; will report after 21 days'.
questions.explanation: Bid-target elasticity tests require modeling marginal auction inventory, marginal CPA, and contribution math. Numerical modeling distinguishes 'worth testing' from 'not worth the risk'.

questions.question: A team has 4 Search campaigns: Brand ($20K, 16x ROAS, 45 leads), Non-Brand-Generic ($50K, 3x, 167 leads), Non-Brand-Long-Tail ($25K, 5x, 50 leads), Competitor ($15K, 1.8x, 25 leads). Total $110K, 287 leads. They want to maximize lead count next month with $30K incremental. Quantify allocation.
answer_criteria: Compute current cost-per-lead: Brand $20K/45 = $444 (mostly non-incremental — these leads would convert organically). Non-Brand-Generic $50K/167 = $300. Non-Brand-Long-Tail $25K/50 = $500. Competitor $15K/25 = $600.

Estimate marginal cost-per-lead at +$X spend (typical decay 20-30%): Brand: marginal CPL very high (Brand is saturated; ~$700+). Non-Brand-Generic: marginal ~$390. Non-Brand-Long-Tail: marginal ~$650. Competitor: marginal ~$780.
Brand is non-incremental — added Brand spend mostly buys conversions that happen anyway. Don't allocate to Brand.
Competitor has lowest current ROI + high marginal CPL. Likely under-performing channel.
Best marginal: Non-Brand-Generic at $390/lead.
Recommended: $25K to Non-Brand-Generic (~64 marginal leads), $5K to Non-Brand-Long-Tail (~8 marginal leads). Skip Brand and Competitor.
Project: 287 current + 72 marginal = 359 leads next month from $140K spend = $390 blended CPL.
Compare to other allocations: '$30K to Brand' = ~43 marginal leads (high CPL); 'all to Non-Brand-Long-Tail' = ~46 marginal leads. Recommended = ~72 marginal leads — 1.5-1.7x more than alternatives.

Stated assumptions: marginal CPL decay at 30%; lead quality is uniform across campaigns (which may not hold — verify if a quality differential exists); Smart Bidding's signal density is sufficient at the higher spend.
Validation plan: implement in 50% steps over 4 weeks; measure marginal CPL at each step; halt if exceeds $500.
Communicate to leadership: 'naïve options under-perform optimal allocation by 30-40% in lead count; recommendation maximizes lead pipeline'.
questions.explanation: Lead-count maximization requires marginal-CPL computation per channel + Brand/Competitor incrementality recognition. The math reveals optimal allocation that intuition misses.

questions.question: A retailer running PMax has $100K monthly spend at 4.5x reported ROAS. Geo holdout test (50% of geos paused for 21 days) showed: pause-geos had $30K less revenue than running geos. PMax-attributed revenue in running-geos: $230K. Quantify the true incremental contribution + recommend.
answer_criteria: Compute test mechanics: 21 days at $100K/30 days = $70K test-period spend across 100% of geos. Pause-geos had 50% normally-attributed PMax revenue forgone = $115K worth of PMax attribution lost.
Actual revenue gap (treatment - control): $30K. This is the true incremental contribution of PMax in pause-geos over 21 days.
Implied incremental ROAS: $30K incremental revenue / $35K test-period PMax spend in pause-geos (50% of $70K) = 0.86x incremental ROAS.
The remaining $115K - $30K = $85K of PMax-attributed revenue would have happened anyway (via brand search, organic, other channels picking up the demand).
Implied: PMax is 26% incremental at current scale ($30K/$115K = 26%).
Strategic implication: PMax is mostly capturing existing demand. At 0.86x incremental ROAS, PMax is barely break-even on a true-contribution basis. Margin contribution may be negative.

Recommended actions: (1) verify the test methodology — was the pause-geo equivalence pre-validated? (2) if the 26% incrementality is real, scale down PMax 30-50% and redirect to higher-incrementality channels (Demand Gen, expanded Non-Brand Search).
Verify: check brand exclusions on PMax. If not enabled, brand cannibalization explains much of the non-incrementality. Enabling brand exclusions could improve incrementality to 50%+.
Stated assumptions: pause-geo equivalence is valid; 21-day test is sufficient duration; no cross-geo contamination.
Validation plan: re-run the test 30 days after implementing brand exclusions to confirm whether the cause was cannibalization vs structural non-incrementality.
Communicate to CFO: 'true incremental ROAS is 0.86x; PMax is barely contributing margin; major restructure or scale-down recommended; running follow-up test after brand exclusion fix'.
