answer_criteria: Foundation: feed quality is the #1 lever in retail Shopping/PMax. Different SKUs have different upside; the scoring system prioritizes by expected impact.

Score each SKU on 4 dimensions: (a) Title quality (0-3): keyword inclusion, structure, length; (b) Image quality (0-3): white background, no text, high resolution; (c) Identifier completeness (0-2): GTIN, MPN, brand; (d) Attribute richness (0-2): color, size, material, gender, age group, product type.
Total score per SKU: 0-10. Higher score = better feed quality.
Cross-reference with revenue contribution: pull 90-day revenue per SKU. Prioritize fix-list = SKUs with high revenue + low quality score.
Top priority list: top 100 revenue SKUs with quality score <6. These are the highest-leverage interventions.
Mid priority: top 500 revenue SKUs with quality score 6-7. Medium-leverage interventions.
Low priority: long-tail SKUs (revenue rank >500). Lower per-SKU impact; bulk-template fixes are appropriate.

Phase 1 — Top priority manual fixes (week 1-3): hand-craft titles + ensure image quality + fill attributes for top 100 SKUs. Expected impact: 20-40% improvement in those SKUs' Shopping/PMax performance.
Phase 2 — Mid priority template-based fixes (week 4-6): apply category-specific title templates + automated attribute filling for top 500.
Phase 3 — Long-tail bulk improvements (month 2-3): automated template application across remaining 7,500 SKUs. Modest per-SKU improvement, large aggregate impact.
Operational: monthly re-scoring as new products launch + as quality interventions complete.
Quality gates: every new product launched must score ≥7 before going live. Prevents future low-quality accumulation.
Long-term: quarterly quality review. Re-score all SKUs; identify quality drift (e.g., product attributes changed, prices outdated).
Documentation: scoring rubric + intervention playbook documented for the team.
Risk: bulk template changes can temporarily disrupt PMax Learning. Stage rollout (top 100 first, 14-day observation, then mid-tier).
Communicate to leadership: 'feed quality is the highest-leverage retail intervention; scoring system + tiered rollout produces 20-40% impression/click lift across the catalog'.
questions.explanation: Feed quality requires scoring + revenue-impact prioritization. Bulk fixes without prioritization waste effort on low-leverage SKUs.

questions.question: Design an experiment to test whether 'mobile call extensions' (click-to-call buttons) drive incremental conversions for a service business (e.g., HVAC, plumbing) running Search ads.
answer_criteria: Pre-state the question: 'Do mobile call extensions drive incremental phone conversions, or do they merely shift click-conversions to call-conversions (zero-sum)?'
Methodology: Google Ads Experiments tool. Cell A — current campaigns with call extensions. Cell B — same campaigns with call extensions disabled. Equal spend per cell over 21 days.
Pre-launch validation: confirm conversion tracking captures both web form submissions AND phone calls (via call-tracking). Without call tracking, the test can't measure phone conversions.
Primary metric: total conversions (web form + phone calls) per cell. Phone calls counted via call-tracking integration.
Secondary metrics: per-conversion-type CPA (web vs phone), call duration, lead quality (if measurable downstream).

Pre-commit decision rule: if Cell A (with call extensions) has >15% more total conversions vs Cell B at equal spend, call extensions are incremental — keep enabled. If similar, call extensions are zero-sum (shifting clicks to calls); operational decision based on lead quality. If Cell A is worse, the extensions distract from conversions; disable.
Watch for: phone conversions often have higher quality (immediate engagement, ready-to-buy intent) than web forms. Even if total volume is similar, phone-call mix may be more valuable.
Lead quality measurement: track downstream conversion of phone leads vs web leads. If phone leads convert to customers at 2x web leads, the extensions add value beyond raw conversion count.
Statistical power: at typical service-business volume, 21 days produces sufficient calls + web conversions for ~15% effect detection.
Confound: call extension serving depends on device/placement. Verify both cells have equivalent device distribution.
Action 1 — design and launch the test.
Action 2 — measure with focus on total conversions + per-channel quality.
Action 3 — based on result + lead quality data: enable / disable / refine call extension strategy.
Action 4 — document the brand's optimal extension portfolio.
Communicate: 'call extensions test is service-business-specific; results inform which extensions add incremental value vs which are operational overhead'.
questions.explanation: Call extension tests require call-tracking infrastructure + lead-quality measurement. The decision incorporates lead value, not just raw count.

questions.question: Design a 'sustainable RSA testing program' for a Google Ads account with 30 ad groups. Cadence: 2-3 net-new RSA tests per month per ad group.
answer_criteria: Foundation: sustainable RSA testing requires brief-flow + production-cadence + decision-rule discipline. Without process, the program decays into ad-hoc edits.
Hypothesis framework: every RSA test starts with a stated hypothesis (e.g., 'we believe pricing-led headlines outperform feature-led for this audience'). One hypothesis per RSA.
Brief template per RSA: hypothesis, target audience, value prop, anti-patterns, success criteria, brief author.
Test cadence: 2-3 new RSAs per ad group per month. With 30 ad groups, that's 60-90 RSA tests/month account-wide.
Production capacity: requires 1 full-time copywriter focused on RSA optimization (or 0.5 FTE if 30 ad groups). Without dedicated resource, the cadence isn't sustainable.
Decision rules — kill: at $X spend with no spend share earned by Smart Bidding (X = ~5x typical ad-group CPA). Kill: clear underperformance (CTR <70% of baseline) at >$X spend.
Decision rules — iterate: 70-110% of baseline = kill but spawn variant in next sprint. Decision rules — scale: >110% of baseline sustained 14+ days = make the new RSA the primary.
Use Ad Variations tool for high-stakes changes: 50/50 split, statistically tested before rolling out.
Learning archive: every test logged with hypothesis + variant + result + tagged learnings. Searchable.
Operational ownership: one creative-strategist as RSA testing owner. Without ownership, the program decays.
Quality gates: every new RSA must (a) include keyword in 2+ headlines, (b) hit 'Good' or 'Excellent' Ad Strength, (c) have at least 4 distinct value-prop angles.
Avoid: changing other variables in parallel (no bid changes, no audience changes, no keyword changes during refresh window).
Documentation: monthly performance report + hypothesis library updates.
Quarterly retrospective: review of validated hypotheses + emerging patterns; refresh brief templates.
Communicate to team: 'sustainable RSA testing is process + ownership + cadence; without all three, the program churns without compounding'.
questions.explanation: Sustainable RSA testing requires hypothesis-driven briefs, production cadence, decision rules, learning archive, and dedicated ownership.

questions.question: Design an experiment to determine whether you should launch a new campaign type (e.g., Demand Gen) for your DTC brand or scale existing PMax instead.
answer_criteria: Pre-state the question: 'For an additional $40K/month budget, should we launch a Demand Gen campaign or scale our existing PMax?'
Methodology: parallel test. Cell A — scale existing PMax by +$40K. Cell B — launch new Demand Gen campaign at $40K. Same products, same audience signals, equal duration.
Test duration: 60-90 days. Demand Gen needs longer runway than PMax to reach steady-state; PMax scaling needs runway for marginal-decay to settle.
Primary metric: total incremental revenue + new-customer count per dollar of incremental spend. Use source-of-truth analytics (Shopify) to measure.
Secondary metrics: brand search volume (halo signal), per-channel ROAS, AOV mix, NCA rate.
Pre-launch validation: ensure PMax scaling cell has audience + creative refresh capacity (don't just add budget without inputs); ensure Demand Gen cell has sufficient creative for launch.

Hypothesis: PMax scaling typically has marginal returns of 75-85% of average ROAS due to saturation. Demand Gen has higher initial Learning cost but stronger upper-funnel halo (can lift downstream PMax + Brand Search performance).

Decision rule: if Demand Gen produces >70% of PMax's expected marginal revenue AND drives measurable brand search lift, Demand Gen is the better long-term investment (compounds over time). If PMax scaling produces equal or better short-term revenue with no clear halo, scale PMax.
Watch for: Demand Gen's first 60 days are high-cost-low-conversion (Learning phase + upper-funnel nature). The 90-day measurement window captures the full-funnel value.
Confound: Demand Gen cell may amplify PMax cell's performance via halo. The test should measure them in isolated geos OR account for cross-channel halo in interpretation.
Action 1 — design parallel test with 90-day window.
Action 2 — measure with full-funnel attribution (not just last-click).
Action 3 — based on result: scale the winner; document the methodology + decision for future budget-allocation decisions.
Action 4 — re-evaluate quarterly; channel performance + saturation curves shift over time.
Communicate to CFO: 'this is a budget-allocation experiment; result reveals which incremental channel produces highest contribution; 90-day measurement window required'.
questions.explanation: Channel-vs-scale tests require parallel implementation + full-funnel measurement + multi-month duration. Halo effects matter for new-channel launches.
